## VISION TRANSFORMERS NEED REGISTERS

**Timoth´ee Darcet[1,2]**

**Maxime Oquab[1] Julien Mairal[2] Piotr Bojanowski[1]**

1 FAIR, Meta

> 2 Univ. Grenoble Alpes, Inria, CNRS, Grenoble INP, LJK, 38000 Grenoble, France

## ABSTRACT

Transformers have recently emerged as a powerful tool for learning visual representations. In this paper, we identify and characterize artifacts in feature maps of both supervised and self-supervised ViT networks. The artifacts correspond to high-norm tokens appearing during inference primarily in low-informative background areas of images, that are repurposed for internal computations. We propose a simple yet effective solution based on providing additional tokens to the input sequence of the Vision Transformer to fill that role. We show that this solution fixes that problem entirely for both supervised and self-supervised models, sets a new state of the art for self-supervised visual models on dense visual prediction tasks, enables object discovery methods with larger models, and most importantly leads to smoother feature maps and attention maps for downstream visual processing.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Embedding images into generic features that can serve multiple purposes in computer vision has been a long-standing problem. First methods relied on handcrafted principles, such as SIFT (Lowe, 2004), before the scale of data and deep learning techniques allowed for end-to-end training. Pursuing generic feature embeddings is still relevant today, as collecting valuable annotated data for many specific tasks remains difficult. This difficulty arises because of the required expertise ( _e.g._ , medical data, or remote sensing) or the cost at scale. Today, it is common to pretrain a model for a task for which plenty of data is available and extract a subset of the model to use as a feature extractor. Multiple approaches offer this possibility; supervised methods, building on classification

or text-image alignment, allow training strong feature models to unlock downstream tasks. Alternatively, self-supervised methods building on the Transformer architecture have attracted significant attention due to their high prediction performance on downstream tasks and the intriguing ability of some models to provide unsupervised segmentations (Caron et al., 2021)

In particular, the DINO algorithm is shown to produce models that contain explicit information about the semantic layout of an image. Indeed, qualitative results show that the last attention layer naturally focuses on semantically consistent parts of images and often produces interpretable attention maps. Exploiting these properties, object discovery algorithms such as LOST (Sim´eoni et al., 2021) build on top of DINO. Such algorithms can detect objects without supervision by gathering information in attention maps. They are effectively unlocking a new frontier in computer vision.

DINOv2 (Oquab et al., 2023), a follow-up to DINO, provides features that allow tackling dense prediction tasks. DINOv2 features lead to successful monocular depth estimation and semantic segmentation with a frozen backbone and linear models. Despite the strong performance on dense tasks, we observed that DINOv2 is surprisingly incompatible with LOST. When used to extract features, it delivers disappointing performance, only on par with supervised alternative backbones in this scenario. This suggests that DINOv2 behaves differently than DINO. The investigation described in this work notably exposes the presence of artefacts in the feature maps of DINOv2 that were not present in the first version of this model. These are observable qualitatively using straightforward methods. Also surprisingly, applying the same observations to supervised vision transformers exposes similar artifacts, as shown in Fig. 2. This suggests that DINO is, in fact, an exception, while DINOv2 models match the baseline behavior of vision transformers.

In this work, we set out to better understand this phenomenon and develop methods to detect these artifacts. We observe that they are tokens with roughly 10x higher norm at the output and correspond to a small fraction of the total sequence (around 2%). We also show that these tokens appear around the middle layers of the vision transformer, and that they only appear after a sufficiently long training of a sufficiently big transformer. In particular, we show that these outlier tokens appear in patches similar to their neighbors, meaning patches that convey little additional information.

As part of our investigation, we evaluate the outlier tokens with simple linear models to understand the information they contain. We observe that, compared to non-outlier tokens, they hold less information about their original position in the image or the original pixels in their patch. This ob-

servation suggests that the model discards the local information contained in these patches during inference. On the other hand, learning an image classifier on outlier patches yields significantly stronger accuracy than doing so on the other patches, suggesting that they contain global information about the image. We propose the following interpretation to these elements: the model learns to recognize patches containing little useful information, and recycle the corresponding tokens to aggregate global image information while discarding spatial information.

This interpretation is consistent with an inner mechanism in transformer models that allows performing computations within a restricted set of tokens. In order to test this hypothesis, we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image. We train several models with and without this modification and observe that the outlier tokens disappear from the sequence entirely. As a result, the performance of the models increases in dense prediction tasks, and the resulting feature maps are significantly smoother. These smooth feature maps enable object discovery methods like LOST mentioned above with the updated models.

## 2 PROBLEM FORMULATION

As shown in Fig. 2, most modern vision transformers exhibit artifacts in the attention maps. The unsupervised DINO backbone (Caron et al., 2021) has been previously praised for the quality of local features and interpretability of attention maps. Surprisingly, the outputs of the subsequent DINOv2 models have been shown to hold good local information but exhibit undesirable artifacts in attention maps. In this section, we propose to study _why_ and _when_ these artifacts appear. While this work focuses on alleviating artefacts in all vision transformers, we focus our analysis on DINOv2.

## 2.1 ARTIFACTS IN THE LOCAL FEATURES OF DINOV2

**Artifacts are high-norm outlier tokens.** We want to find a quantitative way of characterizing artefacts that appear in the local features. We observe that an important difference between “artifact” patches and other patches is the norm of their token embedding at the output of the model. In Fig. 3 (left), we compare the norm of local features for a DINO and DINOv2 model given a reference image. We clearly see that the norm of artifact patches is much higher than the norm of other patches. We also plot the distribution of feature norms over a small dataset of images in Fig. 3 (right), which is clearly bimodal, allowing us to choose a simple criterion for the rest of this section: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as “high-norm” tokens, and we will study their properties relative to regular tokens. This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models. In the rest of this work, we use “high-norm” and “outlier” interchangeably.

**Outliers appear during the training of large models.** We make several additional observations about the conditions in which these outlier patches appear during the training of DINOv2. This analysis is illustrated in Fig. 4. First, these high-norm patches seem to differentiate themselves from other patches around layer 15 of this 40-layer ViT (Fig. 4a). Second, when looking at the distribution of norms along training of DINOv2, we see that these outliers only appear after one third of training (Fig. 4b). Finally, when analyzing more closely models of different size (Tiny, Small, Base, Large, Huge and giant), we see that only the three largest models exhibit outliers (Fig. 4c).

**High-norm tokens appear where patch information is redundant.** To verify this, we measure the cosine similarity between high-norm tokens and their 4 neighbors right after the patch embedding layer (at the beginning of the vision transformer). We illustrate the density plot in Fig. 5a. We observe that high-norm tokens appear on patches that are very similar to their neighbors. This suggests that these patches contrain redundant information and that the model could discard their information without hurting the quality of the image representation. This matches qualitative observations (see Fig. 2) that they often appear in uniform, background areas.

**High-norm tokens hold little local information.** In order to better understand the nature of these tokens, we propose to probe the patch embeddings for different types of information. For that we consider two different tasks: position prediction and pixel reconstruction. For each of these tasks, we train a linear model on top of the patch embeddings, and measure the performance of this model. We compare the performance achieved with high-norm tokens and with other tokens, to see if high-norm tokens contain different information than “normal” tokens.

- **Position prediction.** We train a linear model to predict the position of each patch token in the image, and measure its accuracy. We note that this position information was injected in the tokens before the first ViT layer in the form of absolute position embeddings. We observe that high-norm tokens have much lower accuracy than the other tokens (Fig. 5b), suggesting they contain less information about their position in the image.

- **Pixel reconstruction.** We train a linear model to predict the pixel values of the image from the patch embeddings, and measure the accuracy of this model. We observe again that high-norm tokens achieve much lower accuracy than other tokens (Fig. 5b). This suggests that high-norm tokens contain less information to reconstruct the image than the others.
**Artifacts hold global information.** In order to evaluate how much global information is gathered in the high-norm tokens, we propose to evaluate them on standard image representation learning

This token is then considered as the image representation. We then train a logistic regression classifier to predict the image class from this representation, and measure the accuracy. We observe that the high-norm tokens have a much higher accuracy than the other tokens (Table 1). This suggests that outlier tokens contain more global information than other patch tokens.

## 2.2 HYPOTHESIS AND REMEDIATION

Having made these observations, we make the following hypothesis: _large_ , _sufficiently trained_ models learn to recognize _redundant_ tokens, and to use them as places to _store_ , _process_ and _retrieve_ global information. Furthermore, we posit that while this behavior is not bad in itself, the fact that it happens inside the patch tokens is undesirable. Indeed, it leads the model to discard local patch information (Tab. 5b), possibly incurring decreased performance on dense prediction tasks.

We therefore propose a simple fix to this issue: we explicitly add new tokens to the sequence, that the model can learn to use as registers. We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token. At the end of the vision transformer, these tokens are discarded, and the [CLS] token and patch tokens are used as image representations, as usual. This mechanism was first proposed in Memory Transformers (Burtsev et al., 2020), improving translation tasks in NLP. Interestingly, we show here that this mechanism admits a natural justification for vision transformers, fixing an interpretability and performance issue that was present otherwise.

We note that we have not been able to fully determine which aspects of the training led to the appearance of artifacts in different models. The pretraining paradigm seems to play a role, as OpenCLIP and DeiT-III exhibit outliers both at size B and L (Fig. 2). However, the model size and training length also play important parts, as observed in Fig. 4.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we validate the proposed solution by training vision transformers with additional [reg] register tokens. We evaluate the effectiveness of our approach by a quantitative and qualitative analysis. We then ablate the number of registers used for training, to check that they do not

cause a performance regression, evaluate an unsupervised object discovery method atop our features and finally provide a qualitative analysis of the patterns learnt by the registers.

## 3.1 TRAINING ALGORITHMS AND DATA

As the proposed solution is a simple architectural change, we can easily apply it to any training procedure. We try it on three different state-of-the-art training methods for supervised, text-supervised, and unsupervised learning, shortly described below.

**DEIT-III** (Touvron et al., 2022) is a simple and robust supervised training recipe for classification with ViTs on ImageNet-1k and ImageNet-22k. We choose this method as an example of labelsupervised training as it is simple, uses the base ViT architecture, achieves strong classification results, and is easy to reproduce and modify with our improvements. We run this method on the ImageNet-22k dataset, using the ViT-B settings, as provided in the official repository[1] .

**OpenCLIP** (Ilharco et al., 2021) is a strong training method for producing text-image aligned models, following the original CLIP work. We chose this method as an example of text-supervised training because it is open-source, uses the base ViT architecture, and is easy to reproduce and modify with our improvements. We run the OpenCLIP method on a text-image-aligned corpus based on Shutterstock that includes only licensed image and text data. We use a ViT-B/16 image encoder, as proposed in the official repository[2] .

**DINOv2** (Oquab et al., 2023) is a self-supervised method for learning visual features, following the DINO work. We apply our changes to this method as it is the main focus of our study. We run this method on ImageNet-22k with the ViT-L configuration. We use the official repository[3] .

## 3.2 EVALUATION OF THE PROPOSED SOLUTION

As shown in Fig. 1, we get rid of the artifacts by training models with additional register tokens. In the appendix, we provide additional qualitative results for more images in Fig. 19. In order to quantitatively measure this effect, for each model, we probe the norm of features at the output of the model. We report these norms for all three algorithms with and without registers in Fig. 7. We see that when training with registers, models do not exhibit large-norm tokens at the output, which confirms the initial qualitative assessment.

**Performance regression.** In the previous section, we have shown that the proposed approach removes artifacts from local feature maps. In this experiment, we want to check that the use of register tokens does not affect the representation quality of those features. We run linear probing on ImageNet classification, ADE20k Segmentation, and NYUd monocular depth estimation. We follow the experimental protocol outlined in Oquab et al. (2023). We summarize the performance of the models described in Sec. 3.1 with and without register tokens in Table 2a. We see that when using registers, models do not lose performance and sometimes even work better. For completeness, we also provided the zero-shot classification performance on ImageNet for OpenCLIP (Table 2b), which remains unchanged. Please note that the absolute performance of our OpenCLIP reproduction is lower due to the data source we used.

While one register is sufficient to remove artefacts, using more leads to improved downstream performance.

**Number of register tokens.** As described in Sec. 2.2, we propose alleviating the feature maps’ artifacts by adding register tokens. In this experiment, we study the influence of the number of such tokens on local features and downstream performance. We train DINOv2 ViT-L/14 models with 0, 1, 2, 4, 8 or 16 registers. In Fig. 8, we report the results of this analysis. In Fig. 8 **(top)** , we qualitatively study the attention maps and observe that the visible artifacts disappear when adding at least one register. We then examine in Fig. 8 **(bottom)** performance on downstream evaluation benchmarks, following the protocol from Oquab et al. (2023). There seems to be an optimal number of registers for dense tasks, and adding one brings most of the benefit. This optimum is likely explained by the disappearance of artifacts, leading to better local features. On ImageNet, however, performance improves when using more registers. In all our experiments, we kept 4 register tokens.

## 3.3 OBJECT DISCOVERY

Recent unsupervised object discovery methods rely on the quality and smoothness of local feature maps (Sim´eoni et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023). By leveraging DINO Caron et al. (2021), these methods have significantly surpassed the previous state of the art. However, the algorithm leads to poor performance when applied to modern backbones such as DINOv2 Oquab et al. (2023) or supervised ones Touvron et al. (2022). We posit that this can be alleviated by the method proposed in this work. ## 3.4 QUALITATIVE EVALUATION OF REGISTERS

In this final experiment, we qualitatively probe for the behavior of register tokens. We want to verify if they all exhibit similar attention patterns or whether a differentiation automatically emerges. To this end, we plot the attention maps of the class and register tokens to patch tokens. The result of this visualization is shown in Fig. 9. We see that registers do not have a completely aligned behavior. Some selected registers exhibit interesting attention patterns, attending to the different objects in the scene. While nothing enforced this behavior, their activations had some natural diversity. We leave the study of the regularization of registers for future work.

## 4 RELATED WORK

**Feature extraction with pretrained models.** Using pretrained neural network models for extracting visual features has stood the test of time since the AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) CNN model pretrained on ImageNet-1k (Russakovsky et al., 2015). More recent models have upgraded the same setup with modern architectures, such as ResNets (used in, _e.g._ , DETR, Carion et al., 2020) or even Vision Transformers. As Transformers are easily able to handle different modalities during training, off-the-shelf backbones are now commonly trained on label supervision ( _e.g._ , DeiT-III on ImageNet22k, Touvron et al., 2022) or text supervision (e.g., CLIP (Radford et al., 2021)), providing strong _visual foundation models_ , scaling well with model sizes, and enabling excellent performance on a variety of tasks including detection (Carion et al., 2020) and segmentation (Zheng et al., 2021; Kirillov et al., 2023). In this context, supervision relies on annotations in the form of labels or text alignment; the dataset biases (Torralba & Efros, 2011) are not well characterized, yet they drive

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

learning and shape the learned models. An alternative approach consists of not using supervision and letting the models learn from the data via a pretext task that is designed to require understanding the content of images (Doersch et al., 2015). This self-supervised learning paradigm was explored in multiple methods using Vision Transformers: MAE (He et al., 2022) trains a model at reconstructing pixel values of hidden areas of an image and then applies fine-tuning to address a new task. With a different approach, the self-distillation family of methods (He et al., 2020; Caron et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022) showcase strong performance using frozen backbones, allowing for more robustness to domain shifts for task-specific downstream models. In this work, we focused the analysis on selfsupervised learning, and more specifically on the DINOv2 approach (Oquab et al., 2023), which has shown to be particularly effective for learning local features. We showed that despite excellent benchmark scores, DINOv2 features exhibit undesirable artifacts and that correcting these artifacts in the learning process allows for further improvements in the benchmark performances. These phenomenon is even more surprising as DINOv2 builds upon DINO (Caron et al., 2021), which does not show signs of artifacts. We then further showed that the correction techniques hold for supervised paradigms by testing on DeiT-III and OpenCLIP.

**Additional tokens in transformers.** Extending the transformer sequence with special tokens was popularized in BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). However, most approaches add new tokens either to provide the network with new information as for example [SEP] tokens in BERT, provide opportunity to spend more computation on the input as seen with the tape tokens in AdaTape (Xue et al., 2023), or to gather information in these tokens, and use their output value as an output of the model: for classification, as [CLS] tokens in BERT and ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021); for generative learning, as [MASK] in BERT and BEiT (Bao et al., 2021); for detection, as object queries in DETR (Carion et al., 2020), detection tokens in YOLOS (Fang et al., 2021), and ViDT (Song et al., 2021); or for accumulating information from possibly multiple modalities before decoding, as latent token arrays in Perceivers (Jaegle et al., 2021; 2022). Different to these works, the tokens we add to the sequence add no information, and their output value is not used for any purpose. They are simply registers where the model can learn to store and retrieve information during the forward pass. The Memory Transformer (Burtsev et al., 2020), closer to our work, presents a simple approach to improve transformer models using memory tokens added to the token sequence, improving translation performance. In follow-up work, Bulatov et al. (2022) address complex copy-repeat-reverse tasks. Sandler et al. (2022) extend this line to the vision domain for fine-tuning but observe that such tokens do not transfer well across tasks. In contrast, we do not perform fine-tuning and employ additional tokens during pretraining to improve the features obtained for all tasks downstream. More importantly, our study contributes the following new insight in Sec. 2: the mechanism implemented through memory tokens already appears naturally in Vision Transformers; our study shows that _such tokens allow us not to create but to isolate this existing behavior_ , and thus avoid collateral side-effects.

**Attention maps of vision transformers.** Visualising the attention map from [CLS] token to patch tokens was popularized in DINO (Caron et al., 2021). It was shown there that the attention maps of DINO were clean of artifacts, as opposed to the attention maps of previous vision transformers. Other works have since reported interesting attention maps using various techniques: by modifying the optimisation procedure (Chen et al., 2022), by steering the attention scores towards useful image parts (Shi et al., 2023), by modifying the architecture of the transformer layers (Yu et al., 2024), or by introducing a learnable pooling to produce the [CLS] token (Psomas et al., 2023).

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this work, we exposed artifacts in the feature maps of DINOv2 models, and found this phenomenon to be present in multiple existing popular models. We have described a simple method to detect these artifacts by observing that they correspond to tokens with an outlier norm value at the output of the Transformer model. Studying their location, we have proposed an interpretation that models naturally recycle tokens from low-informative areas and repurpose them into a different role for inference. Following this interpretation, we have proposed a simple fix, consisting of appending additional tokens to the input sequence that are not used as outputs, and have found that this entirely removes the artifacts, improving the performance in dense prediction and object discovery. Moreover, we have shown that the proposed solution also removes the same artifacts present in supervised models such as DeiT-III and OpenCLIP, confirming the generality of our solution.

## A INTERPOLATION ARTIFACTS AND OUTLIER POSITION DISTRIBUTION

We plot in Figure 10 (left) the proportion of outlier tokens, characterized by a norm larger than the cutoff value defined manually, following the distribution of norms shown in Fig. 3 (main text). We make two observations:

First, the distribution has a vertical-striped pattern. We investigate this phenomenon and notice that in the original DINOv2 implementation, during training the position embeddings are interpolated from a 16 _×_ 16 map into a 7 _×_ 7 map, without antialiasing. Propagating unit gradients through such an interpolation function (bicubic resize) leads to the following gradients, shown in Fig. 11. In this work, when producing results with DINOv2 (especially for the results in Tables 2a,3), we always apply antialiasing in the interpolation operator, removing the striping pattern, which gives an updated distribution of outlier positions as shown in Fig. 10 (right).

Second, the outliers tend to appear in areas closer to the border of the feature map rather than in the center. Our interpretation is that the base model tends to recycle tokens in low-informative areas to use as registers: pictures produced by people tend to be object-centric, and in this case the border areas often correspond to background, which contains less information than the center.

## B COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS

Since our proposed fix introduces new tokens, it also increases the number of learnable parameters and the FLOP count of the model. We show in Fig. 12 the relationship between number of registers and increase in model FLOP count and parameter count. We observe that adding registers induces a negligible change in number of parameters, and a slight change in FLOP count. Still, for _n_ = 4 registers, the increase in FLOPs stays below 2%.

## C ANALYSIS OF LOST PERFORMANCE

The results presented in Sec. 3.3 show that adding registers allows us to obtain better object discovery performance with DINOv2 models. The conclusions for the two other models studied in this work could be more crisp. In order to understand why this is so, we qualitatively study the impact of removing artifacts on the intermediate computations in the LOST algorithm. We show the intermediate outputs of LOST for all models on a given input image in Fig. 13.

Adding registers improves the scores and the resulting seed expansion for DeiT-III and DINOv2. This observation is coherent with the improved numbers reported in Table 3. For OpenCLIP, however, the LOST algorithm seems robust to the type of outliers observed in the local features. Adding registers does remove artifacts (as clearly shown in Fig. 20) but does not have much impact on the LOST score. It is also worth noting that OpenCLIP, with or without registers, provides comparable performance to DINOv2 without registers and DeiT-III with registers. The qualitative assessment is coherent with the numbers reported in Table 3.

A surprising observation is that despite the existence of high-norm patches in the output of OpenCLIP models without registers (as seen in Fig. 7), the seed expansion score in Fig. 13 looks smooth. In the LOST experiment with OpenCLIP models, we do not use the features directly, but the values from the computation of attention maps. In Fig. 14, we show the seed expansion score for OpenCLIP models with and without registers for keys, queries and values. We see that artifacts are clearly visible as spots in the background for keys and queries, for the model without registers. As soon as registers are used, the LOST score is focusing on the object, with a smoother score for values. We qualitatively observe that for the OpenCLIP model, the value projection filters out the outliers even without registers. This means that the outliers appear to live in the null space of the value projection layer; the investigation for this phenomenon is left for future work.

## D BEHAVIOR OF MODELS TRAINED WITH REGISTERS

In order to better understand the phenomenon at hand, we examine the question of to what extent did the register tokens ”replace” the high-norm tokens and took on the same role.

In Fig. 15 we compare the distribution of token norms for a model with or without registers. This figure is similar to Fig. 7 but with a finer granularity, as we also plot the norm distribution of individual register tokens and [CLS] tokens. We observe the following: with registers, the norms of patch tokens do not contain outliers anymore, and the high-norm tokens are entirely contained in the set of registers. As a result, we conclude that the behavior leading to high-norm outliers in the model is effectively absorbed in the registers.
An additional interesting observation is that the norms of the registers appear to be quantized, compared to the previous outliers; we leave the investigation of this phenomenon for future work.

## D.2 INFORMATION HELD BY TOKENS

In Fig. 16 we display the positional focus for the class token and the 4 registers of a DINOv2+reg model. We produce these plots by running the model on a random subset of ImageNet-22k, and averaging the attention maps for the corresponding tokens at the last layer. We note that ImageNet-22k contains mostly object-centric images rather than scenes, which explains why the average attention maps correspond to centered blobs.

We make several observations. First, the attention maps for registers can be different of each other; for example, register 3 tends to focus on border areas, while the other registers tend to focus on more centered areas. Register 2 tends to focus slightly more on the upper areas of images that others. This is consistent with Fig. 9, where we show registers focusing on different large areas of the image, suggesting some level of specialization.

Second, by comparing the register maps to the [CLS] token map and to a patch token map, we observe that registers produce maps with a large support area, very similarly to the [CLS] token, and very different of a typical patch token which is more localized. As the [CLS] token is known to carry global information (as proven by the linear probing classification performance): this suggests that registers also carry global information.

## E MASKED AUTOENCODERS

Masked Autoencoding (He et al., 2022) is another common way of pretraining self-supervised models. We observe in Fig. 17 that there are no artifacts in the maps produced by MAE: our hypothesis is that the absence of artifacts is due to the training procedure using only a local loss on the patch tokens, rather than an objective involving global aggregation of information. However, we also note that the performance of MAE models is very low for self-supervised representation learning (75% linear probing performance on ImageNet classification for ViT-Large), preventing it from being used as is, and making fine-tuning a requirement.

## F BEHAVIOR PER ATTENTION HEAD

In this section, we investigate whether the artifacts appear only on the attention maps for specific heads of the last vision transformer block, or for all of them. We show in Fig. 18 the input image along with the attention maps for different heads. We observe that the artifacts appear for all attention heads, despite heads focusing on different areas of the object. We still observe that some heads focus more on artifacts than others.

## G VARIANCE ON TOKEN INFORMATION PROBING

The results presented in table 1 are obtained by taking a random patch token, either normal or outlier. However, the choice of this token adds a significant source of variance in the evaluation. progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical but previously overlooked phenomenon in Vision Transformers (ViTs): the emergence of high-norm outlier tokens that sequester global information at the expense of local spatial fidelity. The quality of the work is exceptional, characterized by a systematic investigative approach that bridges the gap between empirical observation (visual artifacts in DINOv2) and architectural hypothesis (the need for "computational registers"). The clarity of the writing is a major strength; the authors transition seamlessly from identifying the "peaky" attention maps to verifying through linear probing that these outliers discard local data in favor of global context. Perhaps the most significant contribution is the simplicity of the proposed solution—appending register tokens—which not only resolves the artifacts but also restores the utility of state-of-the-art models for dense prediction and object discovery tasks. While the paper stands as a definitive improvement in ViT training stability, the only minor area for further elaboration would be a more detailed theoretical exploration of why these artifacts only manifest in larger models and longer training regimes, though the provided empirical evidence is sufficiently compelling for a conference of this caliber.

comments.title: Unclear handling of register tokens in self-attention
comments.quote: We therefore propose a simple fix to this issue: we explicitly add new tokens to the sequence, that the model can learn to use as registers. We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: While the authors describe adding 'register' tokens similar to the [CLS] token, they do not specify the attention mask used during the forward pass. In standard Vision Transformers, all tokens (patches and [CLS]) attend to each other bi-directionally. If registers are intended to act as a 'global memory' without distorting spatial tokens, it is worth clarifying if there is a specific causal or block-diagonal mask. Additionally, the authors state registers are 'discarded' at the end of the transformer; it is unclear if they are removed prior to the final self-attention block or merely ignored in the output set. Since the final block represents significant feature processing, this distinction is vital for reproducibility and understanding if registers influence the final patch features.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in 'high-norm' threshold and sampling strategy
comments.quote: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as “high-norm” tokens... This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models. [...] From those, we choose a single token at random, either high-norm or normal.
comments.explanation: The authors use an absolute threshold of 150 to identify outliers across different models (DeiT-III, OpenCLIP, DINOv2) which have different embedding dimensions and normalization schemes. The lack of a normalized metric (e.g., standard deviations or percentiles) makes the 'outlier' definition arbitrary. Furthermore, the selection process for 'eligible' tokens in linear probing is underspecified: the paper does not clarify how the 'normal' control group is sampled or how images that lack tokens exceeding the 150-norm threshold are handled. This ambiguity impacts the interpretability of the results in Table 1 and Figure 5b.

comments.title: Mismatched and contradictory values in local information probing
comments.quote: normal 41.7 / 22.8 | outlier 0.79 / 5.09
comments.explanation: In Figure 5b, the metrics for 'position prediction' (top-1 acc and avg. distance) appear swapped or misaligned. The 'avg. distance' metric is marked with a down-arrow (lower is better), but the value for outliers (5.09) is significantly lower than for normal tokens (22.8). This implies outliers are spatially more accurate, flatly contradicting the top-1 accuracy (0.79% for outliers vs 41.7% for normal) and the authors' core hypothesis that outliers discard local information. Additionally, the text describes 'lower accuracy' for pixel reconstruction while the table lists 'L2 error' (where higher error matches the hypothesis), creating a prose-to-table inconsistency in terminology.

comments.title: Contradictory OpenCLIP results and overgeneralized claims
comments.quote: For OpenCLIP, the performance is slighty worse with registers... we observe that adding register tokens makes all models significantly more viable for usage in object discovery.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that registers make *all* models significantly more viable for object discovery. However, Table 3 shows a consistent performance regression for OpenCLIP+reg across all benchmarks (e.g., VOC 2007 Corloc drops from 38.8 to 37.1). This contradicts the 'significantly more viable' generalization. Furthermore, the observation that OpenCLIP's value projection (V) naturally filters out outliers (the 'null space' hypothesis) suggests that adding registers may be redundant or counterproductive for this specific model, yet it is presented as a universal fix.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency in Figure 8 Plots
comments.quote: Figure 8: Ablation of the the number of register tokens... (bottom) : performance on three tasks (ImageNet, ADE-20k and NYUd) as a function of number of registers used.
comments.explanation: In the 'Average of depth tasks' plot in Figure 8, the y-axis is labeled 'rmse' (Root Mean Square Error), where lower is better. The plot shows the error trending *upward* (from ~2.73 to ~2.85) as registers are added. This contradicts the claim in Section 3.2 that performance improves with more registers. It is unclear if the y-axis label is incorrect or if the model's performance on depth tasks actually degrades with the proposed solution.

comments.title: Mathematical interpolation logic inconsistency
comments.quote: notice that in the original DINOv2 implementation, during training the position embeddings are interpolated from a 16 × 16 map into a 7 × 7 map, without antialiasing.
comments.explanation: Standard ViT architectures typically interpolate position embeddings *up* to higher resolutions. An interpolation from 16x16 (256 patches) down to 7x7 (49 patches) represents a major decrease in resolution. Given that DINOv2-g operates on large grids where artifacts occupy ~2% of the sequence, a 49-token sequence is far too small to be the primary context for these artifacts. This description likely contains a typographical error regarding the interpolation direction or grid sizes.

comments.title: Inconsistency in claim of 'fixing' problem for supervised models
comments.quote: We show that this solution fixes that problem entirely for both supervised and self-supervised models
comments.explanation: While registers physically remove the high-norm artifacts from patch tokens, Table 2a shows that for supervised DeiT-III, the downstream performance improvement is negligible (e.g., 84.7 to 84.7 Top-1). The claim that the solution 'fixes the problem entirely' for supervised models is visually true regarding feature maps but misleading regarding quantitative performance gains compared to the self-supervised (DINOv2) results.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Global Information' migration
comments.quote: our study shows that _such tokens allow us not to create but to isolate this existing behavior_ , and thus avoid collateral side-effects.
comments.explanation: The authors demonstrate that outliers in baseline models carry superior global information (Table 1). They claim registers 'isolate' this behavior and are then 'discarded'. If the registers now hold the vital global information and are discarded, it is unclear why classification performance (which requires that information) remains stable or improves. This creates a logical gap: if the global info is moved to the [reg] tokens and the [reg] tokens are ignored at the output, the [CLS] token must either be 're-learning' the information or the information was never truly 'isolated'.

comments.title: Unclear handling of register tokens in self-attention
comments.quote: We therefore propose a simple fix to this issue: we explicitly add new tokens to the sequence, that the model can learn to use as registers. We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: While the authors describe adding 'register' tokens similar to the [CLS] token, they do not specify the attention mask used during the forward pass. In standard Vision Transformers, all tokens (patches and [CLS]) attend to each other bi-directionally. If registers are intended to act as a 'global memory' or 'scratchpad' without distorting the spatial tokens, it is worth clarifying if these registers are visible to the patch tokens in all layers, or if there is a specific causal or block-diagonal mask applied. Given the goal is to prevent 'recycling' of patch tokens, a reader might wonder if patch tokens can attend to registers (retrieving information) or only vice-versa (storing information). Standard ViT behavior implies all-to-all attention, but since this is a structural intervention to fix feature map artifacts, the exact connectivity is a key detail for reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in 'high-norm' threshold across models
comments.quote: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as “high-norm” tokens, and we will study their properties relative to regular tokens. This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models.
comments.explanation: The authors identify a clear bimodal distribution in DINOv2-g (Figure 3) and suggest 150 as a threshold. However, they later apply this analysis to other models like DeiT-III and OpenCLIP, which use different embedding dimensions and normalization schemes. The text correctly notes that the cutoff 'can vary,' but it doesn't provide the logic for scaling this threshold. Since 'high-norm' is used as a binary classifier for the linear probing tasks in Table 1 and Figure 5b, the lack of a normalized metric (e.g., standard deviations from the mean or percentage of max norm) makes the 'outlier' definition somewhat arbitrary when moving between a ViT-B (768 dim) and a ViT-g (1536 dim). A reader might find the results in Table 1 hard to interpret without knowing if the 'outlier' population was selected using the same absolute value of 150 or a model-specific percentile.

comments.title: Inconsistency in claim of 'fixing' problem for supervised models
comments.quote: We show that this solution fixes that problem entirely for both supervised and self-supervised models
comments.explanation: The abstract and Section 2.2 claim the solution 'fixes the problem entirely.' While Figure 7 and the qualitative figures show that register tokens effectively 'sink' the high-norm behavior (removing outliers from the patch tokens), Table 2a shows that for DeiT-III (supervised), the performance improvement is negligible (84.7 to 84.7 Top-1, 38.9 to 39.1 mIoU). For DINOv2, the gains are more substantial. The authors acknowledge later that DINO was already an 'exception' and didn't have the artifacts. The term 'fixes' might be interpreted by a reader as 'improves downstream performance,' which is clearly shown for the self-supervised DINOv2 but is less evident for the supervised DeiT-III beyond visual smoothing of maps.

comments.explanation: Looking at Figure 5b (the table for local information probing), the arrangement of values is mathematically and logically confusing. The 'position prediction' task has two metrics: 'top-1 acc' (higher is better) and 'avg. distance' (lower is better). The values provided are 41.7/0.79 for normal tokens and 22.8/5.09 for outlier tokens. However, the bolding is inconsistent: 0.79 is bolded for 'normal', but it is a distance metric where lower is better, so it is correctly identified as superior. But in the second row, 5.09 is listed under 'avg. distance' for outliers. If 22.8 is the top-1 accuracy for outliers, it is significantly worse than 41.7, which supports the authors' claim. However, the layout suggests that for outliers, the accuracy might be 5.09 and the distance 22.8, which would be a massive contradiction in scale. Given the OCR context, it is most likely that '22.8' and '5.09' are simply swapped or misaligned in the table structure, as an average distance of 22.8 pixels (or units) paired with an accuracy of 5.09% would make more sense for a 'failed' prediction than an accuracy of 22.8% and a distance of 5.09. This makes it difficult to verify the exact magnitude of the 'drop' in local information.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in 'reconstruction' metric for outliers
comments.quote: We train a linear model to predict the pixel values of the image from the patch embeddings, and measure the accuracy of this model. We observe again that high-norm tokens achieve much lower accuracy than other tokens (Fig. 5b).
comments.explanation: The text states they measure the 'accuracy' of the pixel reconstruction model. However, Figure 5b explicitly lists the metric as 'L2 error' (with a downward arrow indicating lower is better). In image reconstruction, 'accuracy' is an informal term, but usually, one reports MSE, PSNR, or L2 error. If the authors followed the L2 error metric in the table (18.38 for normal vs 25.23 for outliers), the outliers have *higher* error, which means *lower* fidelity. The text's use of 'lower accuracy' is a colloquialism for 'higher error,' but since the previous bullet point (position prediction) uses 'top-1 acc' as a literal metric, the shifting definition of 'accuracy' here could briefly mislead a reader looking for a percentage-based score in the table. This is a minor prose-to-table inconsistency.

comments.title: Unspecified 'Linear Probing' sampling strategy for outliers
comments.quote: From those, we choose a single token at random, either high-norm or normal. This token is then considered as the image representation. We then train a logistic regression classifier to predict the image class from this representation, and measure the accuracy.
comments.explanation: The authors define outliers as roughly 2% of the sequence. For the 'normal' baseline in Table 1, they choose a single normal token at random. However, for the 'outlier' evaluation, they do not specify what happens if an image has *no* outlier tokens. Earlier in Section 2.1, they note that outliers only appear in large models (ViT-L and above) and after a certain amount of training. If this evaluation is performed on DINOv2-g (which has them), it is still statistically likely that some images (especially high-complexity ones where information is not 'redundant') might not produce a token exceeding the '150 norm' threshold. If the linear probe is trained/tested only on the subset of images that contain outliers, or if a different selection rule is used when no outlier is present, the comparison to the '[CLS]' token and 'normal' tokens (which exist in every image) might be biased. The methodology for handling images without outliers is critical for reproducibility and for ensuring the 78.5-89.7% accuracy reported in Table 1 is representative of the whole dataset.

comments.title: Possible Table Reference Error
comments.quote: Indeed, it leads the model to discard local patch information (Tab. 5b), possibly incurring decreased performance on dense prediction tasks.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Tab. 5b' to support the claim that the model discards local patch information. However, looking at the provided figures and tables, the data concerning 'Local information probing' (position prediction and pixel reconstruction) is actually located in Figure 5b. While Table 1 exists, it pertains to global information (image classification). Given the OCR context, this is likely a typographical error where 'Fig. 5b' was intended. A reader looking for Table 5b would not find it in the vicinity of this argument, potentially causing confusion during the verification of the evidence for the 'recycling' hypothesis.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Discarded' Register Tokens
comments.quote: At the end of the vision transformer, these tokens are discarded, and the [CLS] token and patch tokens are used as image representations, as usual.
comments.explanation: The authors state that register tokens are 'discarded' at the end of the transformer. However, in Transformer architectures, all tokens in a layer attend to all tokens in the previous layer. If the register tokens are present in the final layer's output before being 'discarded' for the loss/downstream head, they have still influenced the values of the patch tokens through the self-attention mechanism in every layer. If the intent is that they are removed *prior* to the final self-attention block, the word 'discarded' is clear; if they are present during the final block's computation but ignored in the output set, then they have participated in the final feature refinement. This distinction is important for reproducibility because the Final Norm layer and the last Attention block represent a significant portion of the feature processing.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency in Figure 8 Plots
comments.quote: Figure 8: Ablation of the the number of register tokens used with a DINOv2 model. [...] (bottom) : performance on three tasks (ImageNet, ADE-20k and NYUd) as a function of number of registers used.
comments.explanation: In the summary and text, the authors mention that adding registers 'sets a new state of the art... on dense visual prediction tasks.' However, if we look at the 'Average of depth tasks' plot in Figure 8 (bottom-right), the y-axis is labeled 'rmse' (Root Mean Square Error), where a lower value is better. The plot shows the line trending *upward* as the number of registers increases (from ~2.73 to ~2.85). This contradicts the statement in Section 3.2 ('performance on downstream evaluation benchmarks... improves when using more registers') and the general claim of performance improvement. If RMSE increases, performance is degrading. It is possible the y-axis label or the units are inverted, or the description of 'improvement' is overgeneralized from the ImageNet/mIoU results which do show positive slopes.

comments.title: Unexplained Performance Drop in OpenCLIP + Registers
comments.quote: For OpenCLIP, the performance is slighty worse with registers (see Sec. C for analysis).
comments.explanation: The authors claim that register tokens 'alleviate' artifacts and 'make all models significantly more viable for usage in object discovery.' However, Table 3 shows a noticeable performance regression for OpenCLIP+reg compared to the baseline OpenCLIP across all three benchmarks (e.g., 38.8 to 37.1 on VOC 2007). While the authors acknowledge this and point to Appendix C, the main text's narrative suggests that registers are a universal fix for 'interpretable attention maps' and retrieval tasks. Given that Table 2 shows negligible change in classification for OpenCLIP, the drop in LOST performance (which relies on attention map quality) contradicts the hypothesis that smoother/artifact-free maps are inherently 'better' for these specific downstream algorithms in all supervised regimes. The text does not provide a high-level intuition for why text-supervision reacts differently than label-supervision or self-supervision in this specific context.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding feature extraction for LOST
comments.quote: We use values for DeiT and OpenCLIP, and for DINOv2, we use keys. Because the output features may have different conditioning, we manually add a bias to the gram matrix of features.
comments.explanation: To reproduce the object discovery results, the reader needs to know the specific 'values' or 'keys' being referred to (presumably from the Multi-Head Self-Attention layers) and, crucially, the value of the 'manually added bias.' The paper notes that DINOv2 was 'surprisingly incompatible with LOST' and that registers fix this, but the introduction of a manual bias change to the Gram matrix suggests the 'fix' might involve hyperparameter tuning specific to the feature scales of these new models. Without specifying the magnitude or derivation of this bias, it is difficult to determine if the improvement stems solely from the architectural change (registers) or from the specific post-processing adjustment made for the LOST algorithm.

comments.title: Unclear handling of [reg] tokens during evaluation
comments.quote: At the output of the model, only the patch tokens and [CLS] tokens are used, both during training and inference.
comments.explanation: The text states that register tokens are discarded and only patch/CLS tokens are used for representations. However, in Section 2.1, the authors demonstrate that 'outlier' tokens (which registers are designed to replace) contain superior global information for classification (Table 1). If the register tokens successfully 'isolate' this global information away from the patch tokens, as claimed in the 'Additional tokens in transformers' paragraph, then discarding them during inference might actually discard the most potent global features. The paper does not explicitly clarify if the [reg] tokens are ever pooled with the [CLS] token for final classification or if the [CLS] token's role is effectively restored once the 'clutter' of global processing is moved to registers. While Table 2 shows no regression, a reader attempting to reproduce this for a new task might be unsure whether to include register outputs in a global average pooling layer or discard them strictly as 'scratch space'.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding the transition of outlier behavior
comments.quote: our study shows that _such tokens allow us not to create but to isolate this existing behavior_ , and thus avoid collateral side-effects.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that registers 'isolate' an existing behavior (storing global info). However, the evidence in Section 2.1 (Table 1) shows that in models *without* registers, global information is stored in a small subset (2%) of 'outlier' patch tokens. When registers are added, the visual artifacts (high-norm outliers) disappear from the patch tokens. The logical leap is whether the global information previously found in the outliers has moved *entirely* to the registers, or if it has redistributed back to the [CLS] token. If the registers now hold that 'high-norm' global information, the claim that they are 'discarded' and 'not used for any purpose' at the output seems to contradict the goal of preserving that information which the model clearly finds necessary to compute. It leaves a slight ambiguity: if the registers hold the 'global' information and are discarded, why does classification performance (which relies on global info) improve or stay stable?

comments.title: Mathematical interpolation logic inconsistency
comments.quote: notice that in the original DINOv2 implementation, during training the position embeddings are interpolated from a 16 × 16 map into a 7 × 7 map, without antialiasing.
comments.explanation: In Section A, the authors describe an interpolation of position embeddings from 'a 16 × 16 map into a 7 × 7 map'. However, standard Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures (including DINOv2 mentioned in the context) typically operate by interpolating low-resolution pretrained position embeddings *up* to a higher resolution to handle larger input images during fine-tuning or inference. An interpolation from 16x16 (256 patches) down to 7x7 (49 patches) would represent a significant decrease in resolution, which contradicts the typical 'high resolution' visualization mentioned in Figure 1 and the standard use case of DINOv2. While this might describe a specific localized architectural quirk of the 'official DINOv2 implementation' during a specific training phase, the prose implies this is the source of the artifacts seen in the provided figures. If the model is processing standard 224x224 images with patch size 14, it expects a 16x16 grid; interpolating *to* 7x7 suggests a mismatch with the actual sequence lengths discussed elsewhere (where artifacts occupy 2% of the sequence, implying a much larger total sequence than 49 tokens).

comments.title: Ambiguity in the 'null space' claim for OpenCLIP
comments.quote: We qualitatively observe that for the OpenCLIP model, the value projection filters out the outliers even without registers. This means that the outliers appear to live in the null space of the value projection layer
comments.explanation: In Section C, the authors observe that OpenCLIP values (V) seem to 'filter out' outliers unlike keys (K) and queries (Q). They hypothesize that outliers 'live in the null space of the value projection layer.' In a standard Transformer, K, Q, and V are all linear projections of the same hidden state $x$ ($W_k, W_q, W_v$). If the outlier behavior is defined by a 10x higher L2 norm of the hidden state $x$ (as established in Section 2.1), and this norm is suppressed in $V$ but not $K$ or $Q$, it implies specific singular value properties of $W_v$ relative to the outlier direction. However, the paper later states that 'OpenCLIP performance may slightly decrease' with registers (Summary) and 'OpenCLIP... provides comparable performance to DINOv2 without registers' (Section C). There is a logical tension here: if the 'value' projection naturally suppresses the artifacts, the justification for adding registers to OpenCLIP (which the authors admit does not significantly change the LOST score) is weakened, yet they apply it as a universal fix. The 'null space' explanation is presented as a 'left for future work' observation, but it creates a slight inconsistency with the primary claim that these high-norm tokens are inherently disruptive to dense tasks across all models.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Position Prediction and Reconstruction Accuracy
comments.quote: We observe that high-norm tokens have much lower accuracy than the other tokens (Fig. 5b)... We observe again that high-norm tokens achieve much lower accuracy than other tokens (Fig. 5b).
comments.explanation: In Figure 5b (the table), the 'outlier' row shows a position prediction top-1 accuracy of 0.79 and an L2 reconstruction error of 25.23. In Table 5 of Appendix D.2, which evaluates the same metrics for a model without registers (row #registers 0), the 'non-outliers' are reported at 66.3 accuracy. However, Figure 5b reports 'normal' (non-outlier) tokens at 41.7 accuracy. While Figure 5b might be using a different model or subset than Table 5, the discrepancy between 41.7 and 66.3 for the same 'normal' baseline is large and unexplained. Furthermore, Fig 5b lists a 'weighted' or 'average distance' metric for outliers as 5.09 vs 22.8 for normal, but lower is usually better for distance, contradicting the prose claim that outliers hold 'less' information if their distance error were actually lower.

comments.title: Contradictory OpenCLIP Corloc Results
comments.quote: For OpenCLIP, the performance is slighty worse with registers (see Sec. C for analysis). ... we observe that adding register tokens makes all models significantly more viable for usage in object discovery.
comments.explanation: The text in Section 3.3 makes a general claim that registers make *all* models significantly more viable for object discovery. However, Table 3 shows that for OpenCLIP, the Corloc score actually drops across all three benchmarks (VOC07: 38.8 to 37.1; VOC12: 44.3 to 42.0; COCO: 31.0 to 27.9) when registers are added. While the text acknowledges this decline in the preceding sentence, the concluding sentence of that paragraph overstates the success by including OpenCLIP in the 'significantly more viable' generalization. This is logically inconsistent with the provided data.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Outlier Identification Layer
comments.quote: First, these high-norm patches seem to differentiate themselves from other patches around layer 15 of this 40-layer ViT (Fig. 4a).
comments.explanation: While the prose identifies Layer 15 as the emergence point, the provided Summary (and the caption for Figure 4) notes that artifacts emerge 'around the middle layers'. In a 40-layer model (ViT-g), Layer 15 is significantly before the midpoint (Layer 20). If the 'recycling' behavior is a result of global information aggregation which typically happens in later layers, the distinction between 'layer 15' and 'middle' (which suggests ~20) should be clarified, especially since Fig 4a's visualization is needed to confirm if the 'differentiation' is a sharp break or a gradual climb.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Average Distance' Metric Directionality
comments.quote: normal 41.7 / 22.8 | outlier 0.79 / 5.09
comments.explanation: In Figure 5b, the metric 'avg. distance' is marked with a down-arrow (↓), indicating that lower values are better (less error). The 'normal' tokens have a distance of 22.8, while 'outliers' have 5.09. This implies outliers are *better* at being localized to a specific distance, or have lower error in position. However, the top-1 accuracy for outliers is 0.79% (nearly random) vs 41.7% for normal. The text claims outliers hold 'much less' information. If the distance metric is indeed an error metric, the outlier value of 5.09 suggests they are more accurate than normal tokens (22.8), which contradicts the accuracy metric and the authors' hypothesis. This is likely an OCR error or a swapped value in the table, as it undermines the core argument that outliers lose local spatial identity.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table Reference regarding Linear Probing
comments.quote: This table is a detailed view of table 1.
comments.explanation: Looking at the provided passage for Table 6, it claims to be a 'detailed view of table 1' focusing on linear probing of normal vs. outlier tokens. However, the 'Full Paper Context' summary and Section 2.2 explicitly reference 'Tab. 5b' as the source for the data showing that models discard local patch information in outliers. Furthermore, the summary mentions the performance of the three main models (DeiT-III, OpenCLIP, DINOv2) is summarized in 'Table 2a'. Usually, Table 1 in such papers is an introductory overview or a high-level summary. If Table 6 is meant to be the breakdown of the 'Outliers hold significantly less local information' claim, it should likely refer to Table 5 (or whichever table introduced the local vs. global information dichotomy) rather than Table 1. While this might be a simple numbering shift, for a reader trying to reconcile the detailed token analysis with the main text's claims, the reference to Table 1 is confusing as Table 1 likely doesn't contain the baseline for this specific outlier-to-patch comparison.

comments.title: Unspecified method for selecting 'eligible' tokens
comments.quote: As we select the patch tokens randomly among the set of eligible tokens, this adds a source of variability.
comments.explanation: The text mentions a 'set of eligible tokens' for the linear probing comparison between normal and outlier tokens. For a researcher to reproduce this analysis, the criteria for 'eligibility' are critical. The paper summary defines outliers using a 'high-norm criterion' (>150), but it doesn't specify how 'normal' tokens are sampled—whether they are sampled from the same image, the same spatial location across different images, or if they must meet a specific low-norm threshold to be considered the 'control' group. If 'eligible' just means 'any token that isn't an outlier', the spatial distribution (background vs. foreground) might bias the linear probing results more than the architectural 'recycling' behavior itself. Without defining what makes a token eligible for this specific test, the stated variability (the grey standard deviation) is difficult to interpret.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-structured investigation into a previously underexplored artifact in Vision Transformers (ViTs): the emergence of high-norm, semantically inconsistent tokens in feature maps, particularly in background regions, across both supervised and self-supervised models. The authors provide a clear and empirically grounded analysis, using simple yet effective diagnostics—such as token norm distributions and attention map visualizations—to demonstrate that these artifacts are prevalent in strong modern models like DeiT-III, OpenCLIP, and DINOv2, but absent in the original DINO, suggesting a divergence in architectural behavior despite architectural similarities. The core insight—that these outlier tokens appear to sacrifice local fidelity to act as internal "workspaces" for global computation—is intuitive and well-motivated, drawing a thoughtful analogy to computational registers. The proposed solution—appending dedicated register tokens to the input sequence—is elegant in its simplicity and remarkably effective: it eliminates the pathological token behavior, yields smoother and more interpretable attention maps, restores compatibility with downstream methods like LOST, and improves performance on dense prediction tasks. The paper is exceptionally clear in presentation, with strong visual support and logical flow from problem identification to solution validation. However, a key limitation lies in the somewhat incomplete mechanistic explanation: while the existence and utility of the artifacts are convincingly shown, a deeper theoretical understanding of *why* larger or longer-trained models develop this behavior—and *how exactly* the registers alter the model’s internal dynamics—would strengthen the claims. Additionally, ablation studies on the number and initialization of register tokens are present but could be more thoroughly explored. Despite this, the work makes a significant contribution to the interpretability and reliability of vision transformers, with practical implications for feature extraction and unsupervised discovery methods, and its findings may influence future designs of transformer-based architectures in vision.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'outlier tokens disappear entirely' claim
comments.quote: we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image. We train several models with and without this modification and observe that the outlier tokens disappear from the sequence entirely.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'disappear entirely' risks overgeneralization, implying a complete elimination of high-norm tokens across all contexts. While Figure 7 supports the removal of artifactual outlier tokens (defined as norm > 150), the wording may mislead readers into assuming no tokens ever achieve high norms—even semantically meaningful ones. A more precise phrasing would clarify that *artifactual* outliers due to repurposed patch tokens are eliminated. This revision would better reflect the paper's operational definition and avoid overstatement.

comments.title: Underspecified initialization and role of register tokens
comments.quote: we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image. We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: Although register tokens are conceptually clear, key implementation details are missing: initialization scheme (e.g., random, zero, normal), position in the sequence (before/after [CLS]), whether they have dedicated position embeddings, how many are used initially, and whether gradients flow through them. They are referenced analogously to [CLS], but unlike [CLS], they are discarded at output and vary in count (up to 16). These omissions hinder full reproducibility. The reference to Memory Transformers helps but does not replace the need for self-contained specification. Clarification on these points is essential given that register count impacts performance.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'artefact' vs. 'artifact'
comments.quote: The investigation described in this work notably exposes the presence of artefacts in the feature maps of DINOv2 that were not present in the first version of this model.
comments.explanation: The paper alternates between British ('artefact') and American ('artifact') spellings without consistency. Given the rest of the paper uses American English conventions (e.g., 'modeling'), the variation appears accidental. While this does not affect technical meaning, it reduces professionalism and could distract careful readers. The authors should standardize spelling throughout for clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent model size notation in figure vs. text
comments.quote: **----- Start of picture text -----** Input DeiT-III-B DeiT-III-L OpenCLIP-B OpenCLIP-L DINO-B DINOv2-g **----- End of picture text -----**
comments.explanation: Figure 2 labels the model as 'DINOv2-g' (giant), while Section 3.1 states experiments use 'ViT-L' configuration. This creates a discrepancy: either the visualization uses a different model than the ablations, or the text is inaccurate. Since artifacts appear in Large and larger models (per Fig. 4c), showing DINOv2-g for illustration is reasonable. However, the paper fails to clarify that the figure is representative rather than the exact model used. Without this, readers may incorrectly assume all results are from giant-scale models, affecting interpretation of computational scale and generalizability.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of 'output token norm' in Figure 5a
comments.quote: **(a)** : Distribution of cosine similarity between input patches and their 4 neighbors. We plot separately artifact patches (norm of the _output token_ over 150) and normal patches.
comments.explanation: The caption labels patches as 'artifact' based on the *final* output token norm, but measures cosine similarity at the *input* (after patch embedding). This risks implying that high input similarity causes outlier behavior, when in fact the labeling is *post-hoc*. While the analysis is valid—investigating whether input properties predict later outlier status—the causal flow is not directly tested. The presentation could mislead readers about directionality. A clearer caption would distinguish between predictive correlation and causation.

comments.title: Unclear cutoff justification for 'high-norm' tokens
comments.quote: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as “high-norm” tokens, and we will study their properties relative to regular tokens. This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models.
comments.explanation: The threshold of 150 is empirically justified by bimodal distributions (e.g., Fig. 3) for DINOv2, but its use across models (DeiT-III, OpenCLIP, DINOv2) is problematic without normalization. Different architectures and training regimes may scale activations differently, so a fixed threshold risks mislabeling tokens. The authors note the cutoff is model-specific but provide no model-specific values or methodology for determining them. A relative criterion (e.g., percentile-based) or per-model calibration would improve reproducibility and strengthen cross-model claims.

comments.title: Missing specification of patch neighborhood definition
comments.quote: we measure the cosine similarity between high-norm tokens and their 4 neighbors right after the patch embedding layer
comments.explanation: The term '4 neighbors' is ambiguous: it could refer to von Neumann (up/down/left/right) or be a simplification ignoring boundaries. The paper does not specify connectivity type or handling of edge patches. While likely minor for qualitative interpretation, this underspecification prevents exact reproduction of Figure 5a. For methodological rigor, the neighborhood definition should be explicitly stated in the figure caption or methods section.

comments.title: Potential Ambiguity in Use of 'Tab. 5b' When Referring to Figure 5b
comments.quote: Indeed, it leads the model to discard local patch information (Tab. 5b), possibly incurring decreased performance on dense prediction tasks.
comments.explanation: The paper references 'Tab. 5b', but no such table exists; the likely intended reference is Figure 5b, which shows local information probing results for normal vs. outlier tokens. This appears to be a typographical or OCR error. Despite being recoverable from context, incorrect cross-references risk misleading readers and should be corrected for academic precision.

comments.title: Ambiguous Performance Trend in Depth Estimation Across Register Count
comments.explanation: The paper states that 'using more [registers] leads to improved downstream performance', but depth estimation (rmse) shows only marginal improvement and near-saturation beyond 4 registers. While mIoU and ImageNet accuracy show gains, depth performance does not exhibit a strong monotonic trend. The prose slightly overgeneralizes; a more accurate statement would be that additional registers 'generally improve or maintain performance' with greatest gains in segmentation. This prevents overinterpretation of the ablation study.

comments.title: Inconsistent Reporting of OpenCLIP+reg Performance in Object Discovery
comments.explanation: The paper notes a performance drop in OpenCLIP+reg on object discovery (VOC2007 from 38.8 to 37.1) and references 'Sec. C' for analysis, but the justification is absent in the provided text. This contradicts the paper’s broader claim that registers improve or preserve performance. While the drop is acknowledged, its explanation is underspecified, raising concerns about the method’s universality, especially in text-supervised models. Stronger contextualization or a brief explanation would help maintain narrative coherence.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Use of Keys vs. Values for Feature Extraction in Object Discovery
comments.quote: We use values for DeiT and OpenCLIP, and for DINOv2, we use keys.
comments.explanation: The paper uses different attention components (keys for DINOv2, values for others) for feature extraction in LOST without justification. Keys and values encode different aspects of attention, and this choice may confound model comparisons. While a bias correction is applied to the Gram matrix, it doesn’t resolve the architectural asymmetry. The lack of explanation raises concerns that performance differences could be influenced by feature choice rather than model behavior. This methodological detail should be justified or analyzed to ensure valid cross-model interpretation.

comments.title: Inconsistency Between Claimed Number of Registers Used and Ablation Study Trend
comments.quote: In all our experiments, we kept 4 register tokens.
comments.explanation: The authors state 4 registers are used universally, yet ablation shows 1 register suffices to eliminate artifacts and performance plateaus beyond 4 in some tasks. The lack of justification for choosing 4—especially when fewer may suffice—raises concerns about methodological transparency. It risks appearing arbitrary unless tied to a global optimization across tasks. A brief rationale or sensitivity analysis would strengthen reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified use of antialiasing in interpolation
comments.quote: In this work, when producing results with DINOv2 (especially for the results in Tables 2a,3), we always apply antialiasing in the interpolation operator, removing the striping pattern, which gives an updated distribution of outlier positions as shown in Fig. 10 (right).
comments.explanation: The use of antialiasing is well-motivated (removes visual artifacts), but details are missing: whether applied during training/inference, the type (e.g., Gaussian pre-filtering), and scope (all layers or only position embeddings). Since interpolation choices impact high-res ViT behavior, this omission could hinder reproduction, especially in reimplementations of DINOv2. Explicit specification is needed for full method clarity.

comments.title: Contradictory interpretation of OpenCLIP value norm behavior in LOST
comments.quote: Interestingly, the seed expansion map computed using values does not exhibit artifacts with nor without registers.
comments.explanation: The paper states that OpenCLIP values naturally suppress outliers due to the value projection, making registers less critical for that component. However, registers still improve LOST performance, suggesting benefits in keys/queries. The current phrasing may mislead readers into thinking the method is redundant for OpenCLIP. Clarification that registers ensure consistent artifact removal across all attention components (not just values) would better support the universality claim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in token selection for linear probing in Table 4
comments.quote: We observe that adding a register does not significantly modify the scores obtained with the [CLS] or patch tokens. However, the outlier patches are removed, and their behavior is transferred to the newly added register.
comments.explanation: In Table 4, performance is compared between 'outlier patch' (no-register) and 'register' (with-register). However, it’s unclear how the outlier patch is selected: highest-norm token per image? average over all high-norm patches? Multiple outliers may exist per image, but only one register is present, making comparisons potentially unfair. Clarification of token selection methodology is necessary for valid interpretation and reproducibility.

comments.title: Register norm quantization not explained or contextualized
comments.quote: An additional interesting observation is that the norms of the registers appear to be quantized, compared to the previous outliers; we leave the investigation of this phenomenon for future work.
comments.explanation: The authors observe 'quantized' register norms in Fig. 15 but use a strong term that implies discrete-level structure. In neural networks, such clustering can arise from nonlinearities without formal quantization. Without evidence (e.g., sharp peaks at fixed intervals), 'clustered' or 'discretized' would be more cautious. Given the note that this is left for future work, it's not critical, but the language should reflect uncertainty to avoid overinterpretation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in dataset usage for Aircraft probing in Table 4 vs Table 1
comments.quote: We evaluate on the aircrafts dataset, as it showed clear conclusions in the similar table 1.
comments.explanation: The text references 'Table 1' as having shown clear conclusions on the aircrafts dataset, but Table 1 in the main text aggregates results without breaking out Aircraft. The aircraft scores first appear in Table 4. This forward reference is factually incorrect and may mislead readers into expecting prior evidence. It appears to be a narrative or editing error that should be corrected to maintain logical flow.

comments.title: Missing standard deviation values in Table 6 description
comments.quote: Table 6: Image classification via linear probing on normal and outlier patch tokens.
