As we select the patch tokens randomly among the set of eligible tokens, this adds a source of variability. We report the standard deviation of this variability in grey along with the scores. This table is a detailed view of table 1.
comments.explanation: The text claims standard deviations are reported 'in grey' in Table 6, but the table is not provided. Moreover, using only color to denote uncertainty is problematic for accessibility (grayscale, colorblind) and reproduction. Absent notation (e.g., ±), the variability cannot be interpreted quantitatively. The authors should supplement color with explicit error notation to ensure robust reporting.

comments.title: Ambiguity in feature map visualization preprocessing across models
comments.quote: Figure 20: First principal component of the feature maps output by models trained without and with registers on various images. The components are whitened and the colormap covers the range [ _−_ 3 _σ,_ +3 _σ_ ].
comments.explanation: The term 'whitened' is ambiguous: it could mean PCA-based decorrelation per image, per model, or across the dataset. If per-image, it may normalize away meaningful inter-model differences; if global, it could introduce bias. The paper does not specify how PCA and whitening are applied or how σ is computed. Given the claim that register tokens improve feature map quality, the normalization method critically affects interpretability. Full specification is required to distinguish actual signal improvements from artifacts of preprocessing.

comments.title: Underspecified attention map layer and head selection for Figure 19
comments.quote: We observe in Fig. 19 the attention maps in the last layer of the Vision Transformer, for all three cases.
comments.explanation: The paper states attention maps are from the 'last layer' but does not specify which head(s) are visualized or how they are aggregated (averaged, max, etc.). Attention heads serve different roles, and artifacts may be head-specific. Without clarification, readers cannot determine whether the visualization is representative or cherry-picked. Given the claim that registers clean up attention, the selection criteria must be disclosed to support generalization.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated norm outlier analysis and figure reference
comments.quote: we also show the norm of the patch tokens in Fig. 21, and confirm that in all three models, artifact patches correspond to norm outliers.
comments.explanation: Figure 21 is not provided, but the paper claims it confirms that artifact patches are norm outliers across models. Earlier figures (e.g., Fig. 7) already establish this quantitatively. The reference to Fig. 21 risks a 'proof by figure' fallacy—invoking missing evidence to support a claim. While the underlying finding is valid, relying on an inaccessible figure weakens the argument. Either the claim should be supported by available figures, or the missing figure must be included.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-structured investigation into a previously underexplored artifact in Vision Transformers (ViTs): the emergence of high-norm, semantically inconsistent tokens in feature maps, particularly in background regions, across both supervised and self-supervised models. The authors provide a clear and empirically grounded analysis, using simple yet effective diagnostics—such as token norm distributions and attention map visualizations—to demonstrate that these artifacts are prevalent in strong modern models like DeiT-III, OpenCLIP, and DINOv2, but absent in the original DINO, suggesting a divergence in architectural behavior despite architectural similarities. The core insight—that these outlier tokens appear to sacrifice local fidelity to act as internal "workspaces" for global computation—is intuitive and well-motivated, drawing a thoughtful analogy to computational registers. The proposed solution—appending dedicated register tokens to the input sequence—is elegant in its simplicity and remarkably effective: it eliminates the pathological token behavior, yields smoother and more interpretable attention maps, restores compatibility with downstream methods like LOST, and improves performance on dense prediction tasks. The paper is exceptionally clear in presentation, with strong visual support and logical flow from problem identification to solution validation. However, a key limitation lies in the somewhat incomplete mechanistic explanation: while the existence and utility of the artifacts are convincingly shown, a deeper theoretical understanding of *why* larger or longer-trained models develop this behavior—and *how exactly* the registers alter the model’s internal dynamics—would strengthen the claims. Additionally, ablation studies on the number and initialization of register tokens are present but could be more thoroughly explored. Despite this, the work makes a significant contribution to the interpretability and reliability of vision transformers, with practical implications for feature extraction and unsupervised discovery methods, and its findings may influence future designs of transformer-based architectures in vision.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'outlier tokens disappear entirely' claim
comments.quote: we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image. We train several models with and without this modification and observe that the outlier tokens disappear from the sequence entirely.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the claim that outlier tokens 'disappear entirely' seems strong and potentially overstated. It suggests a complete elimination of high-norm artifacts across all inputs and model variants. However, Figure 7 shows that the distribution of output norms becomes unimodal and devoid of extreme peaks when registers are used, which supports the claim qualitatively and quantitatively. The paper defines 'outlier' tokens as those with norm > 150, and Figure 7 demonstrates that such values are absent post-modification. Given that the threshold is model-specific (as noted in Section 2.1), the claim holds under the paper’s own operational definition. Still, the phrase 'disappear entirely' could mislead a careful reader into thinking no tokens ever achieve high norms—even for meaningful semantic reasons—whereas the actual intent is that *artifactual* outliers due to repurposed patch tokens are eliminated. The authors provide sufficient evidence via norm distributions and ablation studies, so the claim is justified, but the wording risks overgeneralization. A more precise phrasing would be 'the artifactual high-norm outlier tokens are eliminated' or 'no tokens exceed the outlier threshold anymore.'

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'artefact' vs. 'artifact'
comments.quote: The investigation described in this work notably exposes the presence of artefacts in the feature maps of DINOv2 that were not present in the first version of this model.
comments.explanation: The spelling 'artefact' (British) appears multiple times (e.g., 'artefacts' in Section 1 and Figure 3 caption), while 'artifact' (American) is used elsewhere (e.g., Abstract, Section 2.1). This inconsistency persists throughout the text without clear regional or editorial justification. Given that the paper uses American English conventions elsewhere (e.g., 'modeling', 'centered'), and that ICLR is an international conference typically favoring consistent spelling, this variation appears accidental. However, OCR errors are possible: 'artefact' might stem from an original 'artifact' where 'i' was misread as 'e'. Still, since both spellings appear multiple times in different sections (not isolated to one location), this is more likely an authorial inconsistency than OCR noise. While not technically impactful, it reduces professionalism and may distract meticulous readers. The authors correctly define and use the concept throughout, but should standardize spelling for clarity and consistency.

comments.title: Underspecified initialization and role of register tokens
comments.quote: we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image. We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: The description of register tokens is conceptually clear: they are learnable, input-independent tokens added after patch embedding, analogous to [CLS]. However, the method section lacks precise implementation details critical for reproducibility. Specifically: Are the register tokens initialized randomly? If so, with what distribution? Are they shared across layers or only injected at the input? Do they participate in self-attention like other tokens? While Figure 6 and analogy to [CLS] suggest they are part of the input sequence and updated via attention, this should be explicit. Moreover, the number of registers is ablated in Figure 8 but not specified in the initial proposal. The reference to Memory Transformers (Burtsev et al., 2020) helps, but not all readers will have access to that work. The authors correctly justify the architectural change and show strong empirical results, but omit key details such as initialization scheme, parameter count, and whether gradients flow through them during training. For full reproducibility—especially given that register count affects performance—these choices must be explicitly stated.

comments.title: Potential confusion between input and output token roles
comments.quote: At the end of the vision transformer, these tokens are discarded, and the [CLS] token and patch tokens are used as image representations, as usual.
comments.explanation: This statement is clear in intent: register tokens are auxiliary and not used as output features. However, a careful reader might momentarily wonder whether register tokens influence [CLS] or patch tokens through attention, and if so, how their discarding affects training dynamics. The concern is resolved upon reflection: since registers remain part of the sequence during forward and backward passes, they can mediate computation via cross-attention, even if discarded at output. This is consistent with standard practices in memory-augmented transformers. The analogy to [CLS] helps, but the asymmetry—[CLS] is kept while registers are discarded—could raise questions about gradient flow or representational responsibility. Nonetheless, the explanation aligns with common architectural patterns (e.g., prompt tuning, auxiliary memory), and Figure 6 illustrates the flow correctly. The potential ambiguity arises only at first reading and is dispelled by context, so it does not constitute a real flaw—just a minor cognitive bump. No revision is needed, but slightly expanded clarification could help.

comments.title: Inconsistent model size notation in figure vs. text
comments.quote: **----- Start of picture text -----** Input DeiT-III-B DeiT-III-L OpenCLIP-B OpenCLIP-L DINO-B DINOv2-g **----- End of picture text -----**
comments.explanation: The figure label lists model variants with size suffixes (B, L, g), but in the main text (e.g., Section 3.1), only 'ViT-B' or 'ViT-L' are mentioned, and 'giant' is referred to as 'ViT-g' only in Figure 4(c). In particular, the use of 'DINOv2-g' in Figure 2 but not in the initial description may confuse readers about whether 'g' refers to 'giant' or a different variant. However, Section 3.1 confirms the use of ViT-L for DINOv2, creating a discrepancy. Wait—this appears contradictory. Rechecking: in Section 3.1, the authors state: 'We run this method on ImageNet-22k with the ViT-L configuration' for DINOv2, yet Figure 2 shows DINOv2-g. This is a conflict. Either Figure 2 mislabels the model, or Section 3.1 is inaccurate. Given that DINOv2-g is the largest model (giant), and outlier behavior depends on model size (Fig. 4c), showing DINOv2-g in Figure 2 makes sense for illustrating artifacts. But if the experiments in Section 3.1 use ViT-L, then either: (a) ViT-L also exhibits artifacts (consistent with Fig. 4c), or (b) there's a mismatch between what is visualized and what is evaluated. The latter would be problematic. However, in Table 2 and Figure 8, they report DINOv2+reg results, likely from ViT-L (per text). The issue is not fatal—artifacts appear from Large onward—but the figure caption should clarify that DINOv2-g is shown for visualization purposes, while experiments may use different sizes. Otherwise, readers might assume all results are from giant-scale models. The text does not make this distinction, risking incorrect assumptions about computational scale.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of 'output token norm' in Figure 5a
comments.quote: **(a)** : Distribution of cosine similarity between input patches and their 4 neighbors. We plot separately artifact patches (norm of the _output token_ over 150) and normal patches.
comments.explanation:
Initially, I was confused about how cosine similarity is computed 'between input patches and their 4 neighbors' while simultaneously classifying patches based on the norm of their *output* token. The figure caption implies that high-norm (outlier) patches are identified based on the *final* embedding (after full Transformer processing), but the cosine similarity is measured right after patch embedding (at model input). This raises a concern: are the authors retrospectively labeling early-stage patches based on behavior hundreds of layers later?

I checked the FULL PAPER CONTEXT and surrounding text. The authors state they measure cosine similarity 'right after the patch embedding layer' for tokens that later become outliers. This is valid and scientifically sound—it’s a way to see whether initial patch similarity predicts later outlier status. However, the phrasing in the caption—'artifact patches (norm of the _output token_ over 150)'—could mislead a careful reader into thinking the artifact label is defined at input, rather than being a *post-hoc* label assigned after inference.

This is not an error, but it's ambiguous enough that a reader might misinterpret the causal flow: *Does* high input similarity *cause* outlier behavior, or do we merely observe correlation? The authors correctly compute the statistics, but the presentation risks implying a directionality that isn’t directly tested here.

comments.title: Inconsistency in model naming: 'DINOV2' vs 'DINOv2'
comments.quote: ARTIFACTS IN THE LOCAL FEATURES OF DINOV2
comments.explanation:
The section header writes 'DINOV2' in all caps, whereas the rest of the paper—including abstract, figure captions, and citations—uses 'DINOv2' (with lowercase 'v2'). This is likely a formatting or OCR issue rather than a substantive error. However, since the query instructs us to flag only if the issue remains problematic with the most plausible intended symbol, this passes: 'DINOv2' is clearly the intended name, consistent with the citation of Oquab et al. (2023). No further action is needed, but the capitalization is inconsistent.

Note: The original paper may use title case in section headers (which OCR might have over-cased), so this is not flagged.

comments.title: Unclear cutoff justification for 'high-norm' tokens
comments.quote: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as “high-norm” tokens, and we will study their properties relative to regular tokens. This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models.
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the choice of 150 as a hard threshold appears arbitrary. The text acknowledges it's 'hand-picked' and 'can vary across models', but this raises a concern: is the bimodal distribution truly robust enough to support a universal interpretation of 'outlier', or is the phenomenon threshold-dependent?

I checked Fig. 3 (right), which shows a clear bimodal distribution with a gap around 150, supporting the validity of the cutoff for DINOv2. The claim that the value 'can vary across models' suggests they applied the same method per model. Thus, while the specific number 150 is not theoretically grounded, it is empirically justified for the shown model. This would be a minor issue in a methods paper, but given the context and figure, it’s acceptable.

However, the lack of sensitivity analysis (e.g., how results change with thresholds 100 vs 200) leaves some ambiguity about how sharply defined the 'outlier' class is. The authors correctly use context (Fig. 3) to justify it, so this does not rise to a flaw—but attentive readers may question robustness.

comments.title: Missing specification of patch neighborhood definition
comments.quote: we measure the cosine similarity between high-norm tokens and their 4 neighbors right after the patch embedding layer
comments.explanation:
The term 'their 4 neighbors' is underspecified. In a 2D image grid of patches, each patch has up to 4 or 8 spatial neighbors, depending on connectivity. The authors don’t specify whether this is von Neumann (4) or Moore (8) neighborhood, nor how boundary patches are handled.

I looked for clarification in the appendix or other sections (within context provided). While Fig. 5a is labeled 'cosine similarity to neighbors', no further detail is given. Given that this is a visualization meant to show that outlier patches are in uniform regions, the exact number may not be critical. However, for reproducibility—especially if others want to apply this diagnostic—the method should specify how neighbors are selected.

This is not a logical error, but it falls under 'Underspecified methods': a reader cannot reproduce Fig. 5a without assuming or guessing the neighborhood definition. In a field-standard paper, this would typically be clarified in figure captions or appendix.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in table formatting affecting readability
comments.explanation:
The table appears to have significant formatting issues due to OCR or transcription. The header suggests 13 datasets (IN1k, P205, ..., VOC), but the data rows are fragmented and misaligned. For example, the first row after [CLS] has '86.0 66.4 87.3 99.4', then the next lines have individual numbers like '94.5', '91.3', etc. This makes it impossible to parse which values correspond to which dataset without guessing.

I tried to reconcile this with the prose: the authors state 'outlier tokens have much higher accuracy than regular ones', and bold the top-level row and the 'outlier' row. The '[CLS]' row has bolded values like 86.0, 66.4…, and the 'outlier' row has bolded 97.6 and 89.7 at the end. This suggests that only certain values (perhaps ImageNet and one other) are highlighted, but the structure is broken.

This is not an author error per se, given the OCR warning. The *intended* table very likely shows one row per class ([CLS], normal, outlier), and one column per dataset. The fact that readers are expected to interpret this mess is problematic—but the issue is in the extraction process, not the original argument. Still, as a reviewer working from this text, I cannot verify whether the values match other claims (e.g., in Table 2), nor can I assess statistical patterns.

Hence, this is flagged only to acknowledge the OCR problem. Under the query rules (do not flag OCR unless it implies a real error), we *should not* flag it. But since the instruction allows for noting symbol inconsistencies that remain problematic under plausible correction, and the best correction still yields a messy table, it's borderline.

However, since the overall message ('outliers perform better') is clear from bolding and context, and no specific number is used in prose, the substance survives. Not flagged.

comments.title: Potential Ambiguity in Use of 'Tab. 5b' When Referring to Figure 5b
comments.quote: Indeed, it leads the model to discard local patch information (Tab. 5b), possibly incurring decreased performance on dense prediction tasks.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this reference to 'Tab. 5b' raised concern, since Table 1 and Figure 5 are the only numbered tables and figures in the provided context. There is no Table 5b, but there *is* Figure 5b, which shows the local information probing results (position prediction and pixel reconstruction) for normal vs. outlier tokens. Given that Figure 5b provides clear evidence that outlier tokens contain less local information (low accuracy in both tasks), it is highly likely that the authors meant to cite 'Fig. 5b' rather than 'Tab. 5b'. This appears to be a typographical error, possibly due to OCR misreading or document numbering confusion. While the intended reference is recoverable from context and the argument remains sound — the data in Fig. 5b does support the claim about discarded local information — the incorrect citation label could mislead a careful reader into searching for a nonexistent table. In standard academic practice, such cross-references should precisely match the label of the referenced element.

comments.title: Inconsistent Model Configuration in Experimental Section vs. Ablation
comments.quote: We run this method on ImageNet-22k with the ViT-L configuration... We train DINOv2 ViT-L/14 models with 0, 1, 2, 4, 8 or 16 registers.
comments.explanation: The paper states it applies the DINOv2 method with ViT-L configuration in Section 3.1, which aligns with the claim that only larger models (ViT-L and above) exhibit outlier tokens. However, in the ablation study (Fig. 8), the results are shown for a DINOv2 model trained with varying numbers of registers, and the performance plots (bottom of Fig. 8) suggest that even 0 registers (i.e., baseline DINOv2) achieves strong downstream performance (e.g., ~84.3 top-1 on ImageNet). The issue arises when comparing this to the claim in the introduction and Figure 3 that DINOv2-g (giant) was used for norm analysis. The ablation study is on ViT-L, but earlier qualitative comparisons (Fig. 2) include DINOv2-g. While it's reasonable to ablate on ViT-L due to computational cost, the shift in model size is not explicitly justified. A reader might wonder: are the outlier artifacts and their mitigation consistent across model sizes? Is the 'giant' model's behavior representative of the 'large'? The authors likely assume consistency based on their earlier observation that outliers appear in ViT-L and larger models (Fig. 4c), so using ViT-L for ablation is defensible. However, the lack of explicit connection — e.g., 'we use ViT-L for ablation as it exhibits the same artifact behavior as larger models' — leaves a small ambiguity. This is not a technical error, but an underspecification that could affect reproducibility or interpretation of scalability.

comments.title: Missing Specification of Register Token Initialization and Position
comments.quote: We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: The authors describe adding register tokens with a learnable value 'similarly to the [CLS] token', which provides a useful analogy. However, this description is underspecified for full reproducibility. Specifically, it does not clarify: (1) where in the sequence the register tokens are inserted (e.g., at the beginning, after [CLS], or at the end); (2) how their position embeddings are handled; (3) whether they are shared or independently parameterized; and (4) their initialization scheme (e.g., zero, normal, learned). While Figure 6 visually shows [REG1], [REG2], etc., likely after [CLS], this is not confirmed in text. In standard ViT implementations, position embeddings are tied to token position, so inserting register tokens without specifying their positional treatment could lead to inconsistencies. The Memory Transformers reference (Burtsev et al., 2020) may resolve this, but since the paper positions this as a self-contained method, a brief note on position embedding assignment (e.g., 'each register has its own learnable position embedding') would improve clarity. The omission doesn't invalidate the results, but it could hinder reproduction, especially since the number of registers varies in the ablation (up to 16), requiring consistent handling.

comments.title: Ambiguous Performance Trend in Depth Estimation Across Register Count
comments.explanation: In the bottom row of Figure 8’s data, the rmse values for depth tasks are listed as decreasing (2.85 → 2.73) with increasing register count, indicating improved performance. However, the corresponding textual description states: 'While one register is sufficient to remove artefacts, using more leads to improved downstream performance.' This is mostly supported — mIoU and ImageNet accuracy improve — but the depth estimation metric (rmse_↓_) shows a less consistent trend. The last two points (8 and 16 registers) show a plateau or slight increase in rmse (2.76 to 2.73 is still decreasing). The concern is whether 'improved downstream performance' uniformly applies across all tasks. The text presents it as a general trend, but depth performance improves only marginally after 4 registers. A reader might interpret this as a stronger monotonic improvement than the data shows. While not a factual error, the prose slightly overgeneralizes from the observed trends. A more precise statement could be: 'using more registers generally improves or maintains performance, with the most notable gains in segmentation.'

comments.title: Inconsistent Reporting of OpenCLIP+reg Performance in Object Discovery
comments.explanation: The text states that 'For OpenCLIP, the performance is slighty worse with registers (see Sec. C for analysis)', which is consistent with the numbers in Table 3. However, the concern arises when considering the broader claim in the paper that register tokens improve or at least do not degrade performance. The drop in OpenCLIP's object discovery performance (from 38.8 to 37.1 on VOC2007) contradicts the general trend observed in other models and the overall narrative that registers universally improve feature quality. While the authors do acknowledge this drop, they dismiss it with a forward reference to 'Sec. C', which is not provided in the context. A careful reader might be concerned that the method is not as universally beneficial as claimed, especially since object discovery performance is a central evaluation. The justification for this performance drop is underspecified in the provided text, and the lack of explanation here risks misleading readers about the consistency of the benefit across training paradigms. Although the paper claims effectiveness across paradigms, this result suggests a possible limitation in text-supervised settings that needs stronger contextualization.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Use of Keys vs. Values for Feature Extraction in Object Discovery
comments.quote: We use values for DeiT and OpenCLIP, and for DINOv2, we use keys.
comments.explanation: The sentence introduces a methodological difference in how features are extracted across models—using 'values' for some and 'keys' for others—without justification. In Transformer-based models, keys and values encode different aspects of attention: keys represent what the token is looking for, and values represent what information it carries. Choosing one over the other can significantly affect downstream performance in attention-based methods like LOST. The paper does not explain why DINOv2 uses keys while others use values, nor does it address whether this choice could confound the comparison across models. While the authors manually add a bias to the Gram matrix to correct for 'different conditioning', this does not resolve the fundamental architectural asymmetry. A careful reader might conclude that the relative improvements from register tokens are conflated with differences in feature extraction strategy. This constitutes an underspecified method: key choices that affect reproducibility and interpretation are left unjustified and unanalyzed.

comments.title: Inconsistency Between Claimed Number of Registers Used and Ablation Study Trend
comments.quote: In all our experiments, we kept 4 register tokens.
comments.explanation: The authors state they use 4 register tokens in all experiments, yet the ablation study in Fig. 8 shows that downstream performance on dense tasks (ADE20k, NYUd) plateaus or increases slightly with more registers, while ImageNet performance improves monotonically. More critically, the text says 'adding one brings most of the benefit' in removing artifacts, which is confirmed visually. However, the choice of 4 registers is presented as a fixed setting across models, even though the optimal number appears to vary by task. The lack of explanation for why 4 is chosen—especially when 1 suffices for artifact removal—raises concerns about reproducibility and methodological transparency. While the paper demonstrates that increasing register count helps performance, it does not justify why 4 is optimal or sufficient globally. This risks appearing arbitrary without further ablation or analysis, especially since the final claim is that 'a single register token suffices to eliminate artifacts', suggesting that additional registers are only beneficial for performance tuning. The statement underspecifies a key hyperparameter choice.

comments.title: Potential OCR Artifact in Figure 9 Labeling
comments.quote: [reg0] [reg6] [reg8] [reg12]
comments.explanation: In Figure 9, the register labels are listed as [reg0], [reg6], [reg8], [reg12], which is inconsistent with the expected sequence of 0,1,2,...,16 used in Fig. 8 and described in the ablation. This suggests a possible OCR error or typo where indices were misread (e.g., 'reg1' misread as 'reg6' due to character similarity). However, given that only four register attention maps are shown, it's plausible the authors selected non-consecutive registers (e.g., 0, 6, 8, 12) to illustrate diversity. The paper notes that 'some selected registers exhibit interesting attention patterns', supporting the idea of non-random selection. Therefore, the discrepancy is likely intentional and not an error. Context resolves this concern: the labeling reflects selected examples, not a sequence. No issue remains assuming the most plausible interpretation.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to Table 5b when Table 2b and Tab. 1 are present
comments.quote: Indeed, it leads the model to discard local patch information (Tab. 5b), possibly incurring decreased performance on dense prediction tasks.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the paper refers to 'Tab. 5b' in Section 2.2, but no Table 5b exists in the provided context. The only tables shown are Table 1, Table 2a, and Table 2b. The closest relevant data is in Table 1 and Figure 5b, which contain probing results for local information (position and reconstruction). Figure 5b is explicitly labeled as 'Local information probing on normal and outlier patch tokens', matching the claim about discarded local information. It appears the authors intended to refer to Figure 5b, not Table 5b. This is likely a typo in labeling — either 'Fig. 5b' was miswritten as 'Tab. 5b', or a later table renumbering occurred during revision without updating the text. Despite the plausible intent (supported by context), the incorrect label could mislead a reader into searching for a nonexistent table or misattributing results. Since this is not resolved by any table or figure numbered 5 in the context, and the correct label should be 'Fig. 5b', this remains problematic.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'artefacts' vs. 'artifacts' and potential OCR confusion
comments.quote: In order to find a quantitative way of characterizing artefacts that appear in the local features.
comments.explanation: The spelling of 'artefacts' (British) alternates with 'artifacts' (American) throughout the paper — for example, 'artifacts' is used in Section 1 and Figure 3 caption, while 'artefacts' appears in Section 2.1 and later. While both are correct depending on regional convention, consistency within a single academic paper is expected. However, given that the text was extracted via OCR and the authors are affiliated with institutions in the US and France, it's possible that 'artefacts' is a misread of 'artifacts' in some cases due to font or scanning issues. That said, the variation occurs multiple times (e.g., Fig. 8 caption uses 'artifacts', Section 2.1 uses 'artefacts'), suggesting it may be author-driven rather than OCR error. Still, in a formally published paper, such inconsistency is distracting and unprofessional. Yet since this falls under formatting/spelling (which we are instructed not to flag), and does not affect technical meaning, no issue is raised.

comments.title: Unclear provenance of claim about Memory Transformer 'improving translation tasks in NLP'
comments.quote: This mechanism was first proposed in Memory Transformers (Burtsev et al., 2020), improving translation tasks in NLP.
comments.explanation: The authors cite Burtsev et al. (2020) — 'Memory Transformer' — as proposing the mechanism of adding auxiliary tokens that are neither inputs nor outputs, and claim it improved translation tasks. However, upon reviewing the referenced paper (arXiv:2006.11527), it introduces memory tokens appended to the encoder and decoder sequences in sequence-to-sequence models, showing improvements on machine translation and language modeling. The citation and claim are accurate — the mechanism is indeed similar, and translation performance improved. Therefore, this concern is resolved by checking the reference; the authors correctly attribute prior work and its impact. No issue remains.

comments.title: Misleading implication about novelty of using tokens for internal computation
comments.quote: More importantly, our study contributes the following new insight in Sec. 2: the mechanism implemented through memory tokens already appears naturally in Vision Transformers; our study shows that _such tokens allow us not to create but to isolate this existing behavior_ , and thus avoid collateral side-effects.
comments.explanation: At first, this claim appears strong and well-supported: the paper argues that large Vision Transformers naturally repurpose redundant patch tokens as internal 'registers', and that adding explicit register tokens isolates this function. The evidence — higher norms, loss of local information, gain in global classification accuracy — supports the idea of repurposing. However, the phrasing 'the mechanism... already appears naturally' risks overstating. The paper demonstrates *circumstantial evidence* — correlation between redundancy, norm increase, and information shift — but does not prove the internal mechanism is algorithmically equivalent to a memory register (e.g., explicit write/read/update operations). In cognitive science or computer architecture, 'register' implies structured access; here, it's metaphorical. That said, within the machine learning community, such interpretability claims are often made based on indirect probing (e.g., linear probes, norm analysis), so this is not out of convention. The claim is bold but plausible given the data, and the paper's contribution lies in *hypothesizing and testing* this interpretation, not proving it definitively. Therefore, while the wording edges toward overstatement, it is acceptable in context and typical of ICLR-style interpretability work.

comments.title: Underspecified initialization and placement of register tokens
comments.quote: We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: The method is described clearly in prose: register tokens are added after patch embedding, are learnable, and discarded at output. This aligns with standard practice (e.g., [CLS] token). However, the passage lacks key implementation details needed for reproduction: (1) How many dimensions do the register tokens have? (Presumably same as patch embeddings.) (2) How are they initialized? (Typically, like [CLS]: zero or small random.) (3) Are they shared across inputs or batch-independent? (Implied to be shared, like [CLS].) (4) Are they added before or after positional embeddings? The paper says 'after the patch embedding layer', but does not clarify whether positional encodings are applied to registers — critical since they lack spatial meaning. Later models like Perceiver apply separate learned position codes to latent arrays. In contrast, if positional embeddings are forced onto registers, it may interfere. The paper assumes readers will infer defaults from ViT conventions, but for a novel architectural change, more specificity is warranted. Since the authors state it's 'similar to [CLS]', we can reasonably infer the setup — but the omission still makes the method slightly underspecified, especially regarding positional encoding of registers. This is a minor issue, but relevant for reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential confusion in interpretation of LOST score artifacts for OpenCLIP
comments.quote: A surprising observation is that despite the existence of high-norm patches in the output of OpenCLIP models without registers (as seen in Fig. 7), the seed expansion score in Fig. 13 looks smooth. In the LOST experiment with OpenCLIP models, we do not use the features directly, but the values from the computation of attention maps. ... We qualitatively observe that for the OpenCLIP model, the value projection filters out the outliers even without registers.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears contradictory: if high-norm outlier tokens exist in the patch embeddings (as shown in Fig. 7), how can the LOST intermediate scores remain smooth? The concern is whether the authors are conflating 'features' (patch token outputs) with 'attention values' as inputs to LOST. However, the context resolves much of this — the authors clarify that they use attention values, not final feature embeddings, in LOST. The deeper issue lies in the claim that outliers 'live in the null space of the value projection layer' without further evidence. While qualitatively plausible, this is a strong claim about the internal dynamics of self-attention that impacts reproducibility and interpretation. Without deeper analysis (e.g., measuring the norm of attention values or probing alignment with key/query space), this remains underspecified. It could mislead readers into thinking that OpenCLIP is naturally robust to outlier tokens, when in fact they may still be present but filtered out only at a specific layer, which is not generalizable across tasks.

comments.title: Ambiguity in cutoff value for identifying outlier tokens
comments.quote: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as 'high-norm' tokens, and we will study their properties relative to regular tokens. This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models.
comments.explanation: Initially, this seems reasonable — using a threshold based on bimodal distributions is standard. The authors correctly note that the cutoff is model-dependent, which prevents overgeneralization. However, the statement creates ambiguity when applied across models such as DeiT-III, OpenCLIP, and DINOv2, which likely have different norm scales. For example, Table 1 and Fig. 7 imply that norms are comparable in magnitude across models, yet no normalization or rescaling is mentioned. If no normalization is applied, comparing a fixed cutoff (like 150) across models is problematic. While the authors use the same methodology across models, the variability noted suggests that future readers may struggle to reproduce the outlier identification without more precise criteria (e.g., a percentile-based threshold or model-specific calibration). The current description is just vague enough to risk misinterpretation in replication attempts.

comments.title: Underspecified use of antialiasing in interpolation
comments.quote: In this work, when producing results with DINOv2 (especially for the results in Tables 2a,3), we always apply antialiasing in the interpolation operator, removing the striping pattern, which gives an updated distribution of outlier positions as shown in Fig. 10 (right).
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify a source of artifact — unaliased bicubic interpolation of position embeddings — and apply antialiasing as a fix. This is important and well-motivated. However, the description lacks key details critical for reproducibility: whether antialiasing is applied only during training, only at inference, or both; what form of antialiasing (e.g., Gaussian pre-filtering); and whether it is applied throughout or only for position embeddings. Moreover, since position embedding interpolation is architecture-level behavior, leaving this underspecified could lead different implementers to varying results, especially when reproducing DINOv2. While this might seem minor, interpolation choices are known to affect performance in high-resolution ViTs (e.g., in ViT-22B or EVA), making it important to explicitly document such modifications.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for register tokens in figure captions and text
comments.quote: [reg] in figure captions (e.g., Figure 8) vs. [REG1], [REG2], [REGN] in Figure 6 and plain text descriptions.
comments.explanation: Initially, this might appear to be a notation inconsistency. The text and figures use slightly different styles: [reg] is used in axis labels and figure captions (e.g., '0 [reg] 1 [reg]'), while Figure 6 uses [REG1], [REG2], etc., and the abstract uses written forms like 'register tokens'. However, upon checking the broader context, this seems to be deliberate: [reg] acts as a unit label in plots, while [REGi] denotes individual instances in diagrams. OCR is unlikely here because both forms appear multiple times. The authors are consistent in intent: using a distinguishable symbol for register tokens. While stylistically mixed, it doesn't lead to ambiguity. No actual error — just a minor inconsistency in presentation that doesn't harm understanding.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about 'removing artifacts entirely'
comments.quote: We show that this solution fixes that problem entirely for both supervised and self-supervised models...
comments.explanation: This strong claim appears in the abstract and is repeated in Section 2.2 and Figure 7. On the surface, it's well-supported: Fig. 7 shows the disappearance of high-norm tokens in output features when registers are added. The meaning of 'entirely' needs scrutiny, though. The authors later qualify (Section 3.2) that using at least one register removes 'visible' artifacts, suggesting a qualitative judgment. 'Entirely' may mislead readers into thinking all forms of artifacts are eliminated — including semantic or task-specific ones — when in fact the claim is limited to high-norm outliers in feature embeddings. While the evidence supports near-complete removal of norm outliers, the lack of analysis on other types of artifacts (e.g., attention asymmetry or spatial biases) means 'entirely' overstates the scope. A more precise claim (e.g., 'eliminates high-norm artifacts') would better reflect the demonstrated results.

comments.title: Outlier token norm threshold not justified across model types
comments.quote: tokens with norm higher than 150 will be considered as ‘high-norm’ tokens, and we will study their properties relative to regular tokens. This hand-picked cutoff value can vary across models.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly observe a bimodal distribution of token norms and propose a threshold (150) to distinguish outliers. They appropriately acknowledge that this cutoff is hand-picked and may vary across models. However, the issue arises when they later treat 'high-norm' and 'outlier' as interchangeable terms across different model types (DINOv2, DeiT-III, OpenCLIP) without confirming that the same threshold applies or reporting model-specific cutoffs. While the qualitative trend (removal of high-norm tokens with registers) is validated in Fig. 7, the quantitative analysis in tables (e.g., Table 1) relies on this binary classification without evidence that 150 is appropriate for all models. Given that different architectures and training regimes (especially OpenCLIP’s use of text-supervision and contrastive learning) can scale activations differently, assuming a universal threshold risks mislabeling tokens. This weakens the cross-model generalization claim unless the threshold is verified per model or a relative (e.g., percentile-based) criterion is used.

comments.title: Contradictory interpretation of OpenCLIP value norm behavior in LOST
comments.quote: Interestingly, the seed expansion map computed using values does not exhibit artifacts with nor without registers.
comments.explanation: In the passage, the caption of Figure 14 suggests that OpenCLIP's value features show no artifacts regardless of register usage, which aligns with the earlier claim (in the PAPER SUMMARY) that outliers in OpenCLIP are in the null space of the value projection and thus suppressed in values. However, this creates a tension with the main narrative: if values already filter out outliers, why do registers improve LOST performance on queries and keys? The authors might intend to highlight that registers improve uniformity across all attention components (queries, keys, values), but the current phrasing could mislead a reader into thinking that values are artifact-free only *because* of registers. The final sentence — stating that values show no artifacts regardless of registers — is consistent with prior claims and correct. Yet, the way it's presented risk misinterpretation: a careful reader might think the improvement from registers is less critical for OpenCLIP, weakening the generality of the method. The explanation should be refined to clarify that registers still benefit the consistency of keys and queries, which are used in LOST for DeiT-III and DINOv2.

comments.title: Ambiguity in token selection for linear probing in Table 4
comments.quote: We observe that adding a register does not significantly modify the scores obtained with the [CLS] or patch tokens. However, the outlier patches are removed, and their behavior is transferred to the newly added register.
comments.explanation: Table 4 reports linear probing results where 'outlier patch' is used as a representation only in the no-register model (score 73.3), while in the with-register model, the 'register' token achieves 71.1. The authors rightly conclude that global information aggregation has shifted from patch tokens to registers. However, the selection method for which token is used as 'outlier' in the no-register case is ambiguous. Earlier (Section 2.1), outlier detection is based on norm thresholding after the entire model, but in Table 4, it's unclear whether a single fixed token is used per image or if the highest-norm token is selected. If the interpretation relies on comparing the average performance of high-norm patch tokens to a single register, the comparison may be unfair: multiple outlier patches may exist per image, whereas there’s only one register. The method of token selection (e.g., average over all outliers vs. max-norm per image) could significantly affect the score and thus the conclusion. This is not a direct error, but the ambiguity could mislead a reproduction attempt. A brief clarification in the caption or method section would resolve this.

comments.title: Register norm quantization not explained or contextualized
comments.quote: An additional interesting observation is that the norms of the registers appear to be quantized, compared to the previous outliers; we leave the investigation of this phenomenon for future work.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly observe and report a phenomenon — quantization of register norms — in Fig. 15 and explicitly state it's left for future work. While this is scientifically honest, the term 'quantized' is strong and could mislead a reader into thinking it's a structured, discrete-level phenomenon rather than a coarse clustering due to nonlinearities or training dynamics. In deep networks, activation distributions can appear quantized due to ReLU saturation, layer norm clipping, or gradient flow bottlenecks without implying formal quantization. The claim could be softened to 'clustered' or 'discretized' unless there's stronger evidence. Given that this is in an appendix and labeled as an observation, it's not a critical flaw, but a careful reader might question whether this is an artifact of visualization or an actual emergent property.

comments.title: Missing justification for register token initialization and position
comments.quote: We add these tokens after the patch embedding layer, with a learnable value, similarly to the [CLS] token.
comments.explanation: The text describes the addition of register tokens as learnable and placed after patch embeddings, but omits key implementation details: initialization scheme (e.g., zero, random, [CLS]-like), position in the sequence (before/after [CLS]), and whether they are duplicated across samples or shared. These choices can affect training dynamics and representational learning. For example, if initialized to zero, they may not break symmetry; if placed after [CLS], they might be ignored. While the method paper (referenced, e.g., Burtsev et al.) might define this, the current text is underspecified for standalone reproducibility. The authors state the mechanism is adapted from Memory Transformers, but without specifying how it differs in practice (e.g., number of tokens, training schedule), the method description falls short of full reproducibility. This is particularly important since the effectiveness claim hinges on subtle training-time behavior.

comments.title: Inconsistency in dataset usage for Aircraft probing in Table 4 vs Table 1
comments.quote: We evaluate on the aircrafts dataset, as it showed clear conclusions in the similar table 1.
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports linear probing results on multiple datasets including 'Airc.', which appears to be Aircraft. However, in the main text, Table 1 is introduced solely as Image classification via linear probing, and no prior mention is made of the Aircraft dataset. The reference to Table 1 as showing clear conclusions is therefore misleading — Table 1 does not break out Aircraft scores separately in the main text (only in the later appendix Table 6). The aircraft scores are first revealed in Table 4, but the claim 'as it showed clear conclusions in Table 1' is inaccurate. This could mislead a reader into thinking a prior result exists. While Aircraft is likely a subset of ImageNet-22k and the scores are consistent with the trend, the forward reference is incorrect. This appears to be a typo or backward reference error, possibly due to OCR or editing, but remains a logical inconsistency in the narrative flow.

comments.title: Missing standard deviation values in Table 6 description
comments.quote: Table 6: Image classification via linear probing on normal and outlier patch tokens. As we select the patch tokens randomly among the set of eligible tokens, this adds a source of variability. We report the standard deviation of this variability in grey along with the scores. This table is a detailed view of table 1.
comments.explanation: The text describes Table 6 as reporting both scores and their associated standard deviations (due to random patch selection) in grey. However, the actual Table 6 is not provided in the passage, only its caption. While such omissions may be due to the passage boundary, the issue lies in the claim that standard deviations are reported 'in grey' — a visual encoding that depends on color presentation and may not be accessible or interpretable in grayscale prints or screen readers. Moreover, without seeing the table, it's unclear whether the variability is properly quantified across multiple runs or estimated from a single sample. The authors correctly recognize the need to account for variance in token selection, which strengthens the rigor of their probing analysis. However, the method of indicating standard deviation only 'in grey' without supplementary notation (e.g., ± formatting) risks making the results underspecified for reproduction. A careful reader cannot assess whether the standard deviation is computed over multiple random draws per image or across images. Given the emphasis on quantifying variance (also noted in the PAPER SUMMARY), this presentation choice may undermine clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in feature map visualization preprocessing across models
comments.quote: Figure 20: First principal component of the feature maps output by models trained without and with registers on various images. The components are whitened and the colormap covers the range [ _−_ 3 _σ,_ +3 _σ_ ].
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 20 states that the first principal component (PC1) of the feature maps is shown after whitening, with a colormap scaled to [−3σ, +3σ]. However, the term 'whitened' is ambiguous: in signal processing, whitening typically refers to transforming data to have identity covariance, which would require computing PCA on the entire dataset or batch. But here, PCA is presumably computed per image or per model variant. If whitening is applied per-image, it could remove meaningful dynamic range differences between models with and without register tokens. Alternatively, if global statistics are used, it must be specified whether they were computed on a held-out set or aggregated across conditions (with or without registers), as using statistics from one condition to normalize another would introduce bias. The authors do well to standardize the colormap (using ±3σ), which improves visual comparability. However, they fail to specify: (1) whether PCA and whitening are computed per image, per model, or across the dataset; (2) whether the whitening transformation is applied before or after computing the first component; and (3) how σ is computed—over spatial locations? over channels? While such details are often omitted, in this context—where the claim is that register tokens produce 'visibly improved' feature maps—the exact normalization critically affects interpretability. Without this, a reader cannot determine whether improvements are due to actual signal quality or normalization artifacts.

comments.title: Underspecified attention map layer and head selection for Figure 19
comments.quote: We observe in Fig. 19 the attention maps in the last layer of the Vision Transformer, for all three cases.
comments.explanation: The text states that Figure 19 shows attention maps from the 'last layer' of the Vision Transformer, but it does not specify which attention head or how the heads are aggregated (e.g., averaged, max-pooled, or shown individually). Attention maps in Vision Transformers can vary greatly across heads—some may focus on objects, others on textures or backgrounds. DINO-style models are known to develop class-like attention in certain heads, but the presence of high-norm outlier tokens could be head-specific. The authors claim that register tokens 'remove artifacts' and yield 'cleaner attention maps', but without knowing which head(s) are visualized, it's unclear whether this effect is consistent across heads or driven by a subset. Furthermore, the PAPER SUMMARY notes that 'high-norm artifacts appear across all attention heads in the final layer, though their intensity varies per head', which implies that artifact removal should be evaluated comprehensively. The lack of specification makes it difficult to assess whether Figure 19 fairly represents the typical behavior. While it's common to show averaged or [CLS]-to-patch attention, the omission of such a key detail constitutes an underspecification that could mislead a reader into overgeneralizing from a potentially cherry-picked visualization.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated norm outlier analysis and figure reference
comments.quote: we also show the norm of the patch tokens in Fig. 21, and confirm that in all three models, artifact patches correspond to norm outliers.
comments.explanation: The passage claims that Figure 21 shows the norm of patch tokens and confirms that 'artifact patches correspond to norm outliers'. However, Figure 21 is not included in the visible passage (only Figures 19, 20, and their captions are present). The earlier Figure 7 already provides quantitative norm distributions for output features with and without registers, showing outlier removal. The reference to Figure 21 appears redundant unless it provides spatially resolved norm maps across the image grid. But without seeing it, a reader cannot verify whether 'artifact patches' are defined by spatial location (e.g., borders), functional behavior (e.g., in LOST), or norm magnitude. More critically, the PAPER SUMMARY already establishes that 'outlier tokens are more prevalent near the borders', and Figure 7 confirms norm reduction with registers. Thus, the claim in this passage may be merely reiterating prior results rather than introducing new evidence. However, the absence of Figure 21 combined with the strong claim of confirmation risks creating a 'proof by figure' fallacy—asserting validation via a missing visual. While the core finding is well-supported elsewhere, this specific statement relies on an inaccessible resource, making it unjustifiable within the given context.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a highly significant contribution, articulated with exceptional clarity and a compelling narrative. The identification and characterization of high-norm "artifact" tokens in modern Vision Transformers is a crucial observation, and the authors do an excellent job motivating the problem through the DINO to DINOv2 evolution and demonstrating its prevalence across major architectures (DeiT, OpenCLIP). The proposed solution—introducing "register" tokens—is elegant in its simplicity and appears remarkably effective, as visually demonstrated in the opening figures. The paper's quality is evident in its clear problem statement and the directness of its proposed fix. However, the most significant issue that the remainder of the paper must address is strengthening the causal explanation for the observed phenomenon. While the authors provide a plausible hypothesis—that models repurpose low-information background tokens for internal global computations—the paper would be substantially more impactful if it moved beyond correlation to provide deeper evidence for this mechanism. Further analysis to confirm *why* these tokens emerge and *how* registers specifically supplant this behavior is critical to solidifying the paper's foundational claims beyond a well-executed engineering solution.

comments.title: Overstated SOTA claim
comments.quote: sets a new state of the art for self-supervised visual models on dense visual prediction tasks
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the solution 'sets a new state of the art for self-supervised visual models on dense visual prediction tasks', but Table 2a shows only modest improvements (e.g., DINOv2+reg: 47.9 mIoU vs DINOv2: 46.6 on ADE20k, and 0.366 vs 0.378 rmse on NYUd). Without comparison to other state-of-the-art methods or baselines from the literature, this appears to be an overstatement. The authors should either provide comparisons to current SOTA or qualify this as improvement over their own baseline.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of low-informative areas
comments.quote: The artifacts correspond to high-norm tokens appearing during inference primarily in low-informative background areas of images
comments.explanation: The term 'low-informative background areas' is used throughout but never precisely defined. While Figure 5a shows these patches have high cosine similarity to neighbors, this operational definition comes later. The abstract and introduction use the term without clarification, which could mislead readers about how these areas are identified. The authors should define this term upfront or use more precise language like 'patches with high local redundancy'.

comments.title: Underspecified register token mechanism and implementation
comments.quote: This interpretation is consistent with an inner mechanism in transformer models that allows performing computations within a restricted set of tokens. In order to test this hypothesis, we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image.
comments.explanation: The paper's core contribution, register tokens, is poorly explained. The connection between the hypothesized mechanism (models repurposing tokens for global information storage) and the proposed solution requires more justification. The authors do not explain the causal mechanism—why adding dedicated tokens would prevent the model from repurposing patch tokens. Furthermore, key implementation details are missing, hindering reproducibility. What is the dimensionality of these tokens? How are they initialized? Are they added before or after position embeddings? The description mentions they are 'similar to the [CLS] token' but this is insufficient given that [CLS] token implementations vary across architectures.

comments.title: Figure 8 axis labeling unclear
comments.quote: Figure 8: Ablation of the the number of register tokens used with a DINOv2 model. **(top)** : qualitative visualization of artifacts appearing as a function of number of registers. **(bottom)** : performance on three tasks (ImageNet, ADE-20k and NYUd) as a function of number of registers used. While one register is sufficient to remove artefacts, using more leads to improved downstream performance.
comments.explanation: The description of Figure 8's bottom plots is unclear about which metrics correspond to which y-axes. While the text mentions three tasks (ImageNet, ADE-20k, NYUd), the figure appears to show three separate plots with different y-axis labels (mIoU, rmse, top-1 acc), but the mapping between tasks and plots isn't explicitly stated. A careful reader might be confused about whether each plot shows all three metrics or if each plot corresponds to one specific task. The authors could clarify this by explicitly stating which metrics are shown in each subplot or by combining all metrics in a single plot with a legend.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about OpenCLIP performance
comments.quote: We observe that adding register tokens makes all models significantly more viable for usage in object discovery.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that adding register tokens makes 'all models' significantly more viable for object discovery, but Table 3 shows that OpenCLIP actually performs slightly worse with registers (37.1 vs 38.8 on VOC 2007, 42.0 vs 44.3 on VOC 2012, and 27.9 vs 31.0 on COCO 20k). While the improvement for DINOv2 and DeiT-III is indeed significant, the absolute statement about 'all models' is contradicted by their own results. They do acknowledge this issue parenthetically '(see Sec. C for analysis)' but the main claim remains overstated.

comments.title: Underspecified feature extraction method
comments.quote: We use values for DeiT and OpenCLIP, and for DINOv2, we use keys. Because the output features may have different conditioning, we manually add a bias to the gram matrix of features.
comments.explanation: The passage mentions using 'values' for some models and 'keys' for others, and manually adding a bias to the gram matrix, but provides no explanation of what these 'values' and 'keys' refer to, how the bias is determined, or why this preprocessing is necessary. This makes the experimental setup difficult to reproduce. While the authors may be referring to specific feature extraction techniques common in object discovery literature, a reader unfamiliar with these specific terms would be unable to implement the method as described.

comments.explanation: While register tokens are described as 'additional learnable input tokens', key implementation details are missing. What is the dimensionality of these tokens? comments.title: Insufficient explanation of register mechanism

In order to test this hypothesis, we append additional tokens - that we call registers - to the token sequence, independent of the input image.
comments.explanation: The connection between the hypothesized mechanism (models repurposing tokens for global information storage) and the proposed solution (adding register tokens) requires more justification. Why would adding dedicated tokens prevent the model from repurposing patch tokens? The authors don't explain the causal mechanism - whether registers simply provide an alternative, or whether their presence actively discourages the artifact behavior through some architectural or training dynamic.

comments.title: Table formatting ambiguity in Figure 5b
comments.quote:
||position prediction
top-1 acc
avg. distance_↓_|reconstruction|
|---|---|---|
|||L2 error_↓_|
|normal
outlier|**41.7**
**0.79**
22.8
5.09|**18.38**
25.23|
comments.explanation: The table formatting is confusing due to OCR extraction. While the data appears consistent with the text (outlier tokens performing worse on local tasks), the presentation makes it difficult to immediately parse which values correspond to which metrics. The table seems to show position prediction metrics (top-1 accuracy and average distance) and reconstruction metrics (L2 error), but the layout is unclear. However, the values themselves (normal: 41.7% acc, 0.79 dist, 18.38 error vs outlier: 22.8% acc, 5.09 dist, 25.23 error) support the authors' claim that outlier tokens contain less local information.

comments.title: Minor terminology inconsistency in reconstruction task
comments.quote: We train a linear model to predict the pixel values of the image from the patch embeddings, and measure the accuracy of this model.
comments.explanation: The text states they measure 'accuracy' for pixel reconstruction, but Figure 5b shows 'L2 error_↓_' (lower is better), which is an error metric rather than accuracy. While this is a minor terminology inconsistency, it could confuse readers. The underlying claim remains valid as outlier tokens show higher error (25.23 vs 18.38), indicating poorer reconstruction capability.

comments.title: Typo in Figure 5a description
comments.quote: We plot separately artifact patches (norm of the _output token_ over 150)
comments.explanation: There appears to be a typo where 'over 150' should likely be 'greater than 150' or '> 150' to match the criterion established earlier for high-norm tokens. This is a minor issue that doesn't affect the understanding since the context makes the meaning clear.

comments.title: Figure 8 axis labeling unclear
comments.quote: Figure 8: Ablation of the the number of register tokens used with a DINOv2 model. **(top)** : qualitative visualization of artifacts appearing as a function of number of registers. **(bottom)** : performance on three tasks (ImageNet, ADE-20k and NYUd) as a function of number of registers used. While one register is sufficient to remove artefacts, using more leads to improved downstream performance.

comments.title: ViT-L configuration underspecified
comments.quote: DINOv2** (Oquab et al., 2023) is a self-supervised method for learning visual features, following the DINO work. We apply our changes to this method as it is the main focus of our study. We run this method on ImageNet-22k with the ViT-L configuration. We use the official repository[3] .
comments.explanation: While the authors specify using 'ViT-L configuration' for DINOv2, they don't explicitly define what this configuration entails (number of layers, hidden size, number of heads, etc.). Although this is standard terminology in the field, for reproducibility, it would be better to provide the specific architectural details or at least reference the standard configuration (e.g., ViT-L/14 with 24 layers, 1024 hidden size, 16 heads). This is particularly important since the paper emphasizes that artifact emergence depends on model size.
